evidence: structure.coupling_matrix has only two cross-directory edges in the entire src tree: src/lib/auth/ -> src/lib/types/ (1) and src/lib/db/ -> src/lib/server/ (1); the second edge comes solely from src/lib/db/connection.ts (dead/unused factory) importing logServerError, so real db modules do not depend on server.

Layer direction is consistent: routes/api/* import from $lib/db, $lib/server, $lib/types, $lib/calculations, $lib/auth; $lib/db, $lib/server, $lib/auth are leaf-style (auth only depends on $lib/types). No cycles surfaced in the directory profiles.

Documented two-database boundary (src/lib/db/schema.ts header comment + connection.ts module doc) is partially eroded at runtime: src/lib/db/index.ts exports getRaidDb AND barrel re-exports the user-DB modules (export * from './userSchema'; './users'; './userRecents'; export { getUserDb } from './userDb'), so '$lib/db' resolves to both databases.

Real consumers split asymmetrically against that barrel: raid handlers use `import { getRaidDb } from '$lib/db'` (7 sites), but user-DB consumers reach past the barrel directly (`$lib/db/userRecents.js`, `$lib/db/bnetAccount`, `$lib/db/userCharacters`, `$lib/db/userDb.js`, `$lib/db/users.js`), so the user side of the barrel exists without an active need and only blurs ownership.

Architecture-contract drift: src/lib/db/connection.ts exports createDrizzlePostgres with full module-doc justifying it as the single factory both wrappers should call, but src/lib/db/index.ts (getRaidDb) and src/lib/db/userDb.ts (getUserDb) inline the same ~15-option postgres() config independently. The contract documents intent that runtime ignores.

Two store roots without a documented boundary: src/stores.ts (apiPopup, dungeonData, wowSummaryStore) sits at top-level src/, while src/lib/stores/ holds recentCharacters/recentReports stores. Calculator components reach the top-level file via `import ... from '../../stores'` (apiForm.svelte, keystoneTable.svelte), creating a 2-level relative import that crosses the lib/non-lib boundary.

'God modules' flagged by the scanner (seo.svelte and layout/footer.svelte at 8 importers each) are intentional shared layout/SEO facades with thin contracts, not coupling hubs that create blast radius for common changes; treating them as architectural hubs would be a false positive.

module_level_io evidence shows side-effect-free imports: hooks.server.ts CSP/cookie reads occur inside request handlers, and api/* request.json() calls are inside RequestHandler bodies, so cross-module coordination via import-time side effects is absent.

data/myWowRoster.ts crosses from $lib/data into $lib/db (bnetAccount, userCharacters); this is a server-side composition pattern (called from auth.ts and api/character-reports), not a UI-layer reach into the DB layer, so the direction stays coherent.

package_size_census reports src as 100% of LOC because there is a single source package; no disproportionate sub-package emerges (db is the largest at 1216 LOC across 10 files, which is proportionate to its responsibility).
README.md is empty (0 bytes) — the top-level project contract a new engineer would read first does not exist; PRODUCT.md/DESIGN.md exist but no entry doc explains what this repo is or how it boots.

src/lib/db/index.ts re-exports the user/auth schema and helpers (`export * from './userSchema'`, `./users`, `./userRecents`, `getUserDb`) alongside `getRaidDb` — collapsing the two-database boundary that `userSchema.ts`'s own header doc is at pains to describe ('two intentionally separate databases ... different Postgres instances behind different connection strings').

src/lib/db/connection.ts defines `createDrizzlePostgres()` and a long doc claiming this module 'owns those settings so the two `getXxxDb()` wrappers in `index.ts` and `userDb.ts` differ only by the env var', but `createDrizzlePostgres` has zero importers — both wrappers still inline the postgres() options, so the documented ownership story is fiction.

src/lib/utils.ts (cn/twMerge helpers, ClassValue/WithElementRef types) and src/lib/utils/ (clientLog.ts) coexist as same-named utility surfaces; the file cannot be read as a barrel for the directory, so `$lib/utils` and `$lib/utils/clientLog` resolve to unrelated concerns under the same name.

Two component roots: src/components/ holds feature components (calculator/, charts/, raid/, layout/, seo.svelte, authButton.svelte) while src/lib/components/ui/ holds shadcn primitives (button/, dialog/, accordion/, …). There is no documented rule for which root receives a new component; src/lib/index.ts is a one-line placeholder comment.

src/lib/hooks/ is empty and src/lib/actions/ contains a single file (reveal.ts) — they read as scaffolded slots from a different conventions playbook that never landed, so newcomers have to guess whether to use them or pick a different home.

src/routes/api/ mixes three naming idioms with no documented rule: event-type resources (boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, death-events, healing-events), provider passthroughs (blizzard, raiderio), and aggregation/verb endpoints (top-players, spec-top-players, spec-statistics, calculate-runs, browse-logs, current-state, recent-characters, my-wow-characters). Locating the right endpoint requires knowing which idiom the author chose.

src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts and src/lib/db/userCharacters.ts have a single importer each (src/lib/data/myWowRoster.ts) — domain code with effectively one consumer sits next to the shared schema/connection plumbing, so the db/ folder mixes 'shared infra' and 'feature-specific data access' under one roof.

Two databases, two schemas (src/lib/db/schema.ts for raid analytics; src/lib/db/userSchema.ts for user/auth), two drizzle configs (drizzle.config.ts, drizzle.user.config.ts), two migrations trees (drizzle/, drizzle/user/) — the duplication is principled and documented inline in userSchema.ts; this is a clear positive ownership pattern within db/ even though index.ts blurs it on export.

src/routes/api/character-reports/+server.ts is in-tree but its directory is flagged as orphaned/aging in past notes; its presence as a 343-line route alongside slimmer siblings dilutes the 'each route owns one resource' read of routes/api.

Naming is uniformly camelCase across src/, lib/auth/, lib/calculations/, lib/data/, lib/db/, lib/server/, lib/stores/, lib/ui/ (244 names sampled, 0 snake_case functions). Verb prefixes are consistent (get/create/fetch/load/is/...).

Module exports are uniformly named exports across all lib/ subdirectories (no default-export islands); class-based modules are limited to specific places (server/, types/, ui/) and used deliberately, not as drift.

Sibling outlier among WCL fight endpoints: src/routes/api/death-events/+server.ts hand-rolls parseFightRequestBody + executeWclQuery + try/catch/WclQueryError/logServerError, while peer endpoints cast-events, damage-events, healing-events, boss-events, damage-average all delegate to the shared handleWclFightRequest helper in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts (which centralises exactly that 400/502/500 contract).

Convention drift in API response field naming: DB-backed endpoints (spec-statistics, spec-top-players, top-players, browse-logs, damage-average, death-hotspots) project snake_case keys (encounter_id, spec_icon, player_name, fight_id, death_count, sample_count) while WCL-backed endpoints (cast-events, damage-events, healing-events, boss-events, death-events) return camelCase keys (castEvents, seriesData, targetID, abilityGameID). Both styles share /api/* with no documented rationale, and downstream consumers (src/routes/api/top-players/+server.ts:160) re-spread the snake_case rows into the response, propagating the drift.

auth/blizzardTokenCache.ts and auth/tokenCache.ts intentionally contain no `type` declaration: they are 17-line config wrappers around the createTokenCache factory in tokenCacheBase.ts, where the shared TokenCacheConfig type lives. The sibling-behavior 'missing type' signal is a false positive of a deliberate factory pattern.

Shared API-response helpers (apiOk/apiError) and logging helpers (logServerError/handleApiError) are reused across both API clusters, so the surface is consistent at the HTTP-shape level even where field-naming and orchestration diverge.

src/lib/server/logger.ts defines logServerError/logServerWarn/handleApiError with a documented '[scope] message' shape and a JSDoc that explicitly tells route handlers to use handleApiError at every top-level catch.

Only 6 of ~14 API route handlers actually use handleApiError (browse-logs, top-players, damage-average, spec-top-players, death-hotspots, spec-statistics); the rest (current-state, recent-reports, recent-characters, player-details, fights, death-events, character-reports, blizzard) inline the documented helper as `logServerError(...) + apiError(...)` — same shape, parallel idiom, against the helper's own docstring.

src/routes/api/character-reports/+server.ts:301 catches ALL thrown errors (auth helper response excepted) and returns `apiOk({ reports: [], guilds: [] })`, indistinguishable from a real empty result — a 200 success masking a 500-class failure.

src/routes/api/blizzard/+server.ts:46-47 logs server-side then returns `apiError(error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Internal Server Error.')`, leaking raw upstream messages (including the full Blizzard error body via `Error: Summary endpoint error: <body>`) to the client; every sibling handler returns a generic message.

WCL upstream failures map to HTTP 502 inside src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts:handleWclFightRequest, but src/routes/api/death-events/+server.ts:101 and src/routes/api/fights/+server.ts:60 inline their own try/catch and return apiError(...) which defaults to 500 for the same WclQueryError class — same failure mode, two different status codes.

src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts:67-74 uses bare `console.error('battlenet: refresh fetch failed')` (no scope tag, error object dropped) and at line 100 has an empty catch (`} catch { /* Ignore persistence failures... */ }`) — server-side code outside src/lib/server/ that bypasses logServerError.

src/lib/data/myWowRoster.ts:95,160,214 uses `console.error('myWowRoster: ...', error)` for three distinct server-side failure paths instead of logServerError, breaking the documented '[scope] message' grep contract; the file's own JSDoc admits 'token, network, and DB errors are swallowed and surfaced as null or via console.error'.

src/lib/utils/clientLog.ts already provides logClientError/notifyClientError with the same scope-prefixed shape, but src/lib/stores/recentCharactersBackends.ts and recentReportsBackends.ts ignore it and use raw `console.error('Error loading recent characters from API:', error)` strings.

Positive: src/lib/data/characterData.ts and warcraftlogs.ts use a deliberate Result-type pattern (`{ kind: 'ok' | 'error' | 'empty' }`) at the network boundary, documented with `@throws Never` in JSDoc — that's a coherent layer choice, not drift.

Positive: src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts defines a typed WclQueryError with kind: 'http' | 'graphql' and discriminates on it in handleWclFightRequest — when the helper is used, callers get a single predictable mapping (400/502/500).
Positive: requireSession returns a tagged `{ response } | { session }` object that every handler discriminates on identically, so authentication failures propagate uniformly without throwing.

Mechanical scan strategy_by_directory shows db/ favors throw (9) and try/catch over return_null, while data/ and stores/ favor return_null and try_catch — that split is plausible (DB layer throws, data/store layer adapts) but several files are tagged 'mixed' (tokenCacheBase, currentState, myWowRoster, wclGraphQL, recentCharactersBackends, recentReportsBackends), confirming intra-file strategy mixing rather than clean layering.

Verb-prefix conventions are consistent across the lib: fetch* for HTTP/data retrieval (fetchReportsForCharacter, fetchRuns, fetchWowSummary, fetchRegion, fetchWithTimeout), get* for synchronous/db lookups (getStoredCharacters, getLastRosterFetch, getUserProfile, getBattleNetAccount, getValidBattleNetUserToken, getRaidDb, getUserDb), and is*/has* for predicates that genuinely return booleans (isTokenExpired, isExcludedBossAbilityEvent, isDefaultVisibleBossAbility) — names predict behavior reliably.

Domain vocabulary is rich and used consistently — wcl/raiderio/blizzard, fight/report/boss/ability, keystone/run/score, oauth/tokenCache, raid-db vs user-db. Boundary helpers like handleWclFightRequest, handleApiError, executeWclQuery name their concern directly rather than using bare verbs.

Event-handler naming uses the framework-standard handle* prefix consistently across UI (handleSignIn/handleSignOut, handleSearch, handleBossChange, handlePageChange, handleSelect, handleReportSelection, handleFightSelection, handleLogSearch, handleBrowsePageChange) — these are conventional UI-event handlers, not generic-verb leakage.

Response variable convention: across at least a dozen call sites (yourReports.svelte, oauthBase.ts, wclGraphQL.ts, myWowCharacters.ts, scoreSources.ts, raid/+page.svelte, logs route) the fetch() result is named `response`. src/components/charts/deathHotspots.svelte:36-41 is the lone outlier using `res`, confirming the historical finding.

src/lib/data/currentState.ts:42 — inside saveToAPI the inner async closure is named `doSave`. The do* prefix conveys nothing the surrounding name (saveToAPI) does not already convey; the closure exists only because the function wraps `if (!browser) return` around an awaitable, so it could be inlined or renamed to a domain verb (persistState/postState).

src/lib/ui/wowheadTooltips.ts:48 — local arrow `const hide = () => { hideTooltipApis(...); hideTooltipElements(...); }`. The closure runs *both* the API hide and the DOM hide as one pass, then is called three times (immediate / rAF / setTimeout). The bare name `hide` hides this multi-step retry-pass intent at the call sites; a name like `runHidePass` or `purge` would communicate the schedule pattern.

Concern signals checked: no generic process*/manage*/run*/do* verbs leak into production domain code beyond the single doSave closure (the only `process*` hit is processLargeDataset inside src/lib/__tests__/performance.test.ts, which is a test fixture and out of scope).

Files are named in camelCase consistently within src/lib (oauthBase.ts, blizzardTokenCache.ts, wclGraphQL.ts, deathHotspots.svelte, abilityMetadata.ts, classColors.ts) matching the holistic_context.conventions.naming_by_directory data showing camelCase dominance per directory.
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: Three confirmed local naming nits remain open (doSave closure, res vs response in deathHotspots, hide closure obscuring multi-step intent). Each is a one-file rename, but together they show that naming review has not been swept end-to-end — small generic-verb / abbreviation outliers persist in otherwise consistent modules.

evidence: src/lib/db/_helpers.ts: dbOperation<T>(label, fn) is a 6-line try/catch-log-rethrow wrapper used consistently by every exported db function in src/lib/db/* (users.ts, userRecents.ts, bnetAccount.ts, etc.) — gives a single, predictable internal shape to db calls.

src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts: handleWclFightRequest centralises body validation + GraphQL execution + error mapping for every per-fight endpoint; src/routes/api/boss-events/+server.ts, cast-events, damage-events etc. are tiny declarative wrappers around it (<25 LOC of logic).

src/lib/server/scoreSources.ts: each source is a small object literal with a focused lookup() method; helpers (colorToHex, roundTenths, titleCaseWords, unique, fetchWithTimeout) are small, single-purpose, and co-located. fetchWithTimeout uses try/finally to always clearTimeout — precise edge-case handling.

src/lib/data/characterData.ts: fetchRuns/fetchWowSummary use discriminated unions ({kind:'ok'|'empty'|'error'}) instead of nullable returns, with explicit try/catch around the network call only — narrow, intention-revealing control flow.

src/lib/stores/recentReportsBackends.ts and recentCharactersBackends.ts: each backend is a small object literal implementing a 2-method interface; AddRecentReportOutcome discriminates 'ok'|'unauthorized'|'unavailable' so callers don't re-derive states from status codes.

src/routes/api/top-players/+server.ts: contains two large duplicated .select({...10 columns...}) blocks (lines ~63-83 and ~85-101) that differ only in the presence of .limit(2000). The dedup loop populates `duplicatesFound: string[]` (line ~107) which is never read or returned. An `if (results.length > 0) { /* Sample record available for debugging if needed */ }` block (line ~104) is dead. `playerMap` is typed `Map<string, any>` and result objects are accessed as `result[metricKey]`. Role filtering walks HEALER_SPECS/TANK_SPECS twice via `.some(... .toLowerCase())` per row instead of a Set.

src/routes/api/character-reports/+server.ts: GUILD_RECENT_REPORTS_QUERY and GUILD_REPORTS_QUERY (lines ~67-104) are byte-identical GraphQL strings. fetchReportsForGuild loops over [GUILD_RECENT_REPORTS_QUERY, GUILD_REPORTS_QUERY], but its catch block does `return []` on the first failure — so the 'fallback to second query' the loop pretends to implement never executes, and even if it did, both queries are the same.

src/routes/api/browse-logs/+server.ts: `let whereConditions = []` (untyped, never reassigned), `let query = ...` (never reassigned), `let filteredLogsData = compositionsData.map(...)` then conditional `.filter` reassignment — `let` used where `const` would suffice and clarify mutation intent.

src/hooks.server.ts: handleSession (lines 39-52) self-fetches `${event.url.origin}/auth/session` and returns null on non-ok with no error handling around the fetch itself (a thrown fetch will propagate); the local CSP construction inline-builds an 11-entry array each request instead of being a module constant.
scan_evidence.systemic_patterns: async_no_await flagged across 29 files — most are SvelteKit RequestHandler signatures required to be async, which is framework-mandated and not low-level sprawl.

src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts: getValidBattleNetUserToken reads bnet account, validates expiry with EXPIRY_BUFFER_SECONDS, refreshes if needed, re-reads to capture rotated scope — sequence is linear, each branch has a clear early-return; no defensive sprawl.

src/lib/data/myWowRoster.ts: fetchRegion uses AbortController + 4500ms timeout, try/finally clearTimeout, ignores per-region failures with `return []` so partial roster is still useful — bounded, explicit side-effects.

src/components/raid/logBrowser.svelte: healerOptions is built once with a nested for-in loop and an inline allowlist `['Restoration','Preservation',...]` — readable for a fixed-size domain list (no over-extraction).
src/lib/utils.ts: cn() is a 1-line wrapper (twMerge(clsx(...))) — wrapper exists for ergonomics and is the codebase's standard className helper, not pass-through bloat.

Shared seam library is well-designed: apiError/apiOk in src/lib/server/apiResponses.ts unify the response shape; logServerError/handleApiError in src/lib/server/logger.ts unify the error log shape; requireSession in src/lib/server/requireSession.ts returns a {session}|{response} discriminated union used by all protected handlers (recent-reports, recent-characters, current-state, my-wow-characters, character-reports, profile/+page.server.ts).

WCL fight endpoints compose cleanly through handleWclFightRequest in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts: parseFightRequestBody handles body validation in one place, executeWclQuery owns the transport, WclQueryError lifts upstream errors into a typed boundary, and route files (boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, healing-events) read as one expression: handleWclFightRequest(...).

Token-cache and DB-client construction are correctly factored into createTokenCache (src/lib/auth/tokenCacheBase.ts) and createDrizzlePostgres (src/lib/db/connection.ts) — the seams that should consolidate near-duplicated provider plumbing exist.

However, createDrizzlePostgres has zero callers: src/lib/db/index.ts (getRaidDb) and src/lib/db/userDb.ts (getUserDb) both still inline the same ~20-line postgres() options block — the seam is built but the two collaborators don't compose with it (already open historical issue).

src/hooks.server.ts handleSession plumbs the auth↔locals seam over HTTP: it does `fetch(${event.url.origin}/auth/session, { headers: { cookie: ... }})` from inside the hook instead of calling the @auth/sveltekit auth() helper directly. Every authed request pays a localhost round-trip and re-parses cookies (already open historical issue).

src/routes/api/death-events/+server.ts re-implements the same parseFightRequestBody → executeWclQuery → transform → WclQueryError-mapped 502/500 sequence inline, duplicating the seam that handleWclFightRequest was extracted to own. Sibling fight handlers (boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, healing-events) all delegate; this one is the lone holdout.

DB-backed handlers each own an ad-hoc row→DTO mapper inline: src/routes/api/damage-average/+server.ts, spec-statistics/+server.ts, spec-top-players/+server.ts, top-players/+server.ts, death-hotspots/+server.ts, browse-logs/+server.ts, current-state/+server.ts, recent-reports/+server.ts, recent-characters/+server.ts each call `.select({ snake_case_alias: drizzleColumn, ... })` and/or `.map(row => ({ snake_case_field: row.x }))`. The DB-naming-convention boundary leaks into 8+ route files and there is no shared mapper layer.

Body-validation seams diverge between siblings: fights/+server.ts, player-details/+server.ts and the WCL fight handlers narrow `await request.json().catch(() => null)` as `unknown` before trusting fields and return apiError('Invalid...', 400); browse-logs/+server.ts casts `await request.json()` directly to `BrowseLogsParams` (already open historical issue).

src/routes/api/raiderio/+server.ts does not wrap its outbound fetch in try/catch and never calls logServerError — diverges from peer external-fetch handlers (blizzard/+server.ts, character-reports/+server.ts) which all use `try { ... } catch (error) { logServerError(scope, ...); return apiError(...) }`. A network throw will bubble as an unstructured 500 with no scoped log line.

Two equally-valid error-translation forms coexist in route catch blocks: `return handleApiError(scope, error, msg)` (browse-logs, damage-average, spec-statistics, spec-top-players, top-players, death-hotspots) and `logServerError(scope, ...); return apiError(msg)` (current-state, recent-reports, recent-characters, blizzard, fights, death-events, player-details, character-reports). Outcome is identical, so the seam is consistent in shape but not in code style — new routes copy whichever neighbour they saw.
Only 11 test files exist for the entire codebase (src/lib/__tests__/*.ts and src/components/__tests__/*.ts).

src/lib/calculations/keystoneCalculations.ts — the entire scoring engine that powers /rating-calculator, the product's headline feature — has zero tests despite well-defined pure functions (scoreFormula, computeRunLevelsForScore).

All 22 SvelteKit API handlers under src/routes/api/**/+server.ts are untested: no request-validation tests, no upstream-failure tests, no error-path coverage. Several (browse-logs, current-state, recent-characters, recent-reports) cast `await request.json()` directly to typed shapes without runtime validation, so silent acceptance of malformed bodies is unverified.

Auth/token modules (src/lib/auth/blizzardOauth.ts, oauth.ts, oauthBase.ts, blizzardTokenCache.ts, tokenCache.ts, tokenCacheBase.ts) have no tests — token caching, expiry, refresh and OAuth client_credentials handshake are all unverified.
src/hooks.server.ts handles CSP headers and Auth.js session attachment for every request; it has no test.

Genuinely useful unit tests exist but are isolated: abilityMetadata.test.ts (icon URLs, grouping logic), warcraftlogs.test.ts (URL extractor), interactionUtils.test.ts (reveal action + IntersectionObserver), utils.test.ts (cn). These are real behavior tests but cover a small fraction of the codebase.
There is no test infrastructure for HTTP handlers — no msw, no mocked fetch wrapper, no SvelteKit RequestEvent test helper — which would make API-handler tests cheap to add.
No snapshot tests are used (good — avoids the snapshot-overuse failure mode), but the absence of integration coverage means there is no end-to-end safety net either.

Strong shared foundations: every src/routes/api/*/+server.ts uses apiOk/apiError from $lib/server/apiResponses, giving a single error envelope `{error: string}` and a uniform success path; auth-protected routes uniformly funnel through requireSession (recent-characters, recent-reports, current-state, my-wow-characters, character-reports).

All public WCL fight endpoints (boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, healing-events) are funnelled through handleWclFightRequest in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts, so they share identical body validation (parseFightRequestBody), error mapping (502 on WclQueryError, 500 otherwise), and success wrapping.

Response field-naming convention is split: DB-derived endpoints emit snake_case (`time_seconds`, `avg`, `std`, `n`, `ci`, `encounter_id` in damage-average; `time_seconds`, `death_count`, `sample_count` in death-hotspots; `player_name`, `spec_icon`, `damage_done`, `report_url`, `report_code`, `fight_id` in spec-top-players and top-players; `report_code`, `fight_id`, `encounter_name`, `healer_specs` in browse-logs), while non-DB endpoints emit camelCase (`targetScore`, `totalScore`, `breakdown` in calculate-runs; `score`, `color`, `source` in character-score; `castEvents`, `abilities` in boss-events; `characterName`, `realm`, `region` in recent-characters; `sourceCharacter`, `timestamp`, `code`, `title` in character-reports). Within a single endpoint (top-players, spec-top-players) query parameters are camelCase but the JSON body is snake_case — a developer cannot predict response field names from one example.

Error-shape contract is broken in src/routes/api/character-score/+server.ts: when params are missing or `region` is invalid the handler returns `apiOk({score:null, color:null, source:null}, 400)` — the success body shape with a 400 status, instead of the codebase-wide `{error:string}` envelope. Clients that branch on `body.error` will silently mis-handle this 400.

Sibling external-proxy endpoints have inconsistent error handling: src/routes/api/blizzard/+server.ts wraps everything in try/catch with logServerError + apiError; src/routes/api/raiderio/+server.ts has no try/catch around `fetch(apiUrl)` or `response.json()`, so a network failure or non-JSON body becomes an unhandled SvelteKit 500 with no log line.

Body-validation rigor varies for POST endpoints. WCL endpoints use parseFightRequestBody / explicit type guards (boss-events, fights at lines 39-44, player-details at lines 26-32). Auth-gated mutators destructure JSON without any validation: src/routes/api/recent-characters/+server.ts POST does `const { characterName, realm, region } = await request.json();` (no checks); src/routes/api/recent-reports/+server.ts POST does `const { code, title, guild, owner } = await request.json();`; src/routes/api/current-state/+server.ts POST destructures `{ urlParams }` then trusts URLSearchParams parsing.

HTTP-method convention drift for read operations: read-only DB endpoints use GET (damage-average, death-hotspots, spec-statistics, top-players, calculate-runs, character-score). Read-only WCL proxy endpoints use POST (boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, healing-events, death-events, fights, player-details). browse-logs is also POST but reads only from the local DB. There's no documented rule, and a developer touching a new endpoint cannot predict the verb.

blizzard endpoint leaks raw upstream error strings into apiError: `apiError(error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Internal Server Error.')` where `error.message` may include `"Summary endpoint error: <full Blizzard JSON>"`, while peer endpoints (raiderio, fights, player-details) return generic strings — clients see different error message granularity per provider.
All public exports are async RequestHandler — no mixed sync/async on the API surface, no overloaded handlers, no public functions with >5 params (handleWclFightRequest takes a single options bag).

spec-top-players accepts `fightFilter='kills_no_deaths'` and `'kills_all'` which both reduce to `eq(specPerformance.isKill, true)` (lines 33-44), while the sibling top-players +server.ts handles `'kills_no_deaths'` distinctly via `and(isKill=true, deathCount=0)`. Same parameter value means different things in two endpoints that share the table.

Single shared helper src/lib/server/requireSession.ts is used by every user-scoped API route: character-reports, current-state (GET+POST), my-wow-characters, recent-characters (GET+POST), recent-reports (GET+POST). All 10 handlers follow the identical 'const auth = await requireSession(locals); if ("response" in auth) return auth.response; const userId = auth.session.user.id;' pattern.
All 401 responses go through apiError('Not authenticated', 401) inside requireSession, so clients see one consistent shape ({error}) and status across the protected surface.

User identity for writes is always taken from auth.session.user.id, never from the request body — no IDOR surface. e.g. recent-characters POST (line 36-38) and recent-reports POST (line 41-46) both pass auth.session.user.id into addUserRecent.

Page-level protection at src/routes/profile/+page.server.ts uses event.locals.getSession with throw redirect(302, '/auth/signin'), which is the appropriate per-layer divergence (page → redirect, API → 401) rather than a convention violation.

Public, unauthenticated API routes (top-players, spec-top-players, spec-statistics, browse-logs, boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, healing-events, death-events, death-hotspots, damage-average, fights, player-details, character-score, calculate-runs, blizzard, raiderio) read public WoW analytics from the shared raid DB or proxy public upstreams keyed by query params (name/realm/region) — no user-scoped state is ever read or written, so the absence of auth is principled rather than a gap.

Auth model is intentionally single-bit (signed-in vs anonymous): there are no roles, permission strings, RLS policies, or service-role bypasses anywhere in the codebase, which removes whole classes of magic-string and admin-bypass inconsistencies the rubric warns about.

Input-validation trust boundary is uneven across siblings: the three authenticated POST handlers destructure request.json() with no validation or try/catch around parsing — current-state/+server.ts:50 (`const { urlParams }: Omit<CurrentState, 'timestamp'> = await request.json();`), recent-characters/+server.ts:40 (`const { characterName, realm, region } = await request.json();`), recent-reports/+server.ts:45 (`const { code, title, guild, owner } = await request.json();`) — while public POST siblings under the same routes/api/ tree consistently use the safer pattern `await request.json().catch(() => null)` plus a parse helper (death-events/+server.ts:66, fights/+server.ts:38, player-details/+server.ts:26).

hooks.server.ts re-implements event.locals.getSession by issuing an in-process fetch to '/auth/session' (handleSession, lines 39-50). This is fragile but uniform — every consumer (requireSession, layout, profile page) reads via the same locals.getSession?.() optional-chain, so the indirection does not create per-route inconsistency.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Auth gating itself is uniform via requireSession, but user-input validation at the same trust boundary is not: authenticated POST handlers (current-state, recent-characters, recent-reports) accept any JSON shape without parse-error handling or runtime checks, while their public POST siblings (death-events, fights, player-details) consistently apply `request.json().catch(() => null)` plus a typed parser. Closing that gap with a small shared body-parsing helper would make the trust boundary symmetrical and earn a higher score.

evidence: src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts exposes handleWclFightRequest, which is consumed by every per-fight WCL endpoint (boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, healing-events, death-events, etc.) — the route files are 30-80 LOC, contain only the GraphQL string + transform, and have no copy-pasted try/catch/error-shape boilerplate.

Positive: SvelteKit framework-imposed layout (src/routes/api/<endpoint>/+server.ts, +layout.svelte, hooks.server.ts) is followed without deviation, so the bulk of the route tree maps predictably to URLs.

src/lib/db/index.ts and src/lib/db/userDb.ts defer all Postgres client construction behind getRaidDb()/getUserDb() factory functions; env vars (DATABASE_URL, DATABASE_USER_URL) are read inside the factory, not at module scope.
src/lib/db/connection.ts centralizes the postgres-js client config and includes an explicit `if (building) throw new Error(...)` guard so a build-time import never opens a connection.

src/lib/auth/tokenCacheBase.ts createTokenCache() is the single source of truth for OAuth client-credentials caching. Module-level state (`cachedToken`, `inFlight`) lives inside a closure; readCredentials is a deferred callback so env.WCL_CLIENT_ID / env.BLIZZARD_CLIENT_ID are not read at import time. The doc-comment explicitly justifies the per-isolate singleton choice.

src/auth.ts wraps SvelteKitAuth in an async config factory `SvelteKitAuth(async () => { ... })`, so env.AUTH_BATTLENET_ID/SECRET/AUTH_SECRET are pulled when SvelteKit invokes the factory rather than at import. Framework bootstrap pattern (skip per rubric).
src/hooks.server.ts is a thin sequence(handleAuth, handleSecurity, handleSession) and contains no import-time side effects.
src/stores.ts only exports `writable(...)` initializations and a pure `new Dungeons()` (synchronous loop populating an in-memory array) — no I/O, no env, no boot order dependency.

src/lib/data/myWowRoster.ts declares a module-level `const rosterCache = new Map(...)` as a documented per-isolate cache layered above the DB; no other module mutates this map and there is no creation-order dependency.
Only one direct `process.env` read in src (`src/auth.ts` line 62 `process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'` for cookie.secure); all other env access goes through `$env/dynamic/private` consistently.
No top-level `await`, no `await import(...)` workarounds, and no circular-init lazy-import patterns found across src/lib (grep for `await import(` outside tests returned nothing).

The SvelteKitAuth factory destructures into module-level exports `export const { handle, signIn, signOut } = SvelteKitAuth(...)` — a one-time framework bootstrap. The dependent modules (auth/tokenCache, db/userDb) all expose lazy getters, so the destructure does not chain expensive init.
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: Minor convention drift: src/auth.ts reads `process.env.NODE_ENV` directly inside the cookie config while the rest of the codebase consistently uses `$env/dynamic/private`. In Cloudflare Workers, `process.env` is shimmed but not the documented contract — same value could be sourced via `dev` or env-aware helper. Also the SvelteKitAuth factory pattern means a single failing dependency import (e.g. logger -> apiResponses) would taint the whole hook chain; a per-handle late-bind would be more isolation-friendly but is unidiomatic for SvelteKit Auth. Neither rises to a real defect.

evidence: src/lib/db/index.ts (getRaidDb) and src/lib/db/userDb.ts (getUserDb) duplicate a ~30-line postgres() config block (prepare:false, max:1, idle_timeout:5, connect_timeout:10, transform:undefined, types:{}, max_lifetime:30, etc.) verbatim except for the env-var name and the 'User' string in error messages. src/lib/data/characterData.ts:42 declares `@throws Never — network failures are surfaced as { kind: 'error', status: 0 }` for fetchRuns, but `await response.json()` is performed outside the try/catch that wraps fetch — a malformed JSON body would propagate a SyntaxError, contradicting the documented never-throws contract. contract_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: Two public data fetchers document `@throws Never` but leave JSON parse outside the try/catch (characterData.ts), one auth helper claims persistence in its docstring but silently swallows DB errors (bnetAccount.refreshBattleNetToken), and the db/index.ts barrel exports a partial subset of the DB API while no caller actually consumes it. None are systemic — the codebase otherwise shows unusually disciplined contract documentation (dbOperation, requireSession, ScoreSource, AddOutcome) — but the inaccurate `@throws Never` claims undermine the trust the rest of the documentation earns.

logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: Two confirmed dead-code/dead-branch defects (character-reports duplicate query loop, top-players empty-if-with-comment), one always-undefined declared variable (rating-calculator seoTitle), and a recurring stylistic 'async wrapper, no await' pattern across 5 SvelteKit route handlers/loads. Each is local but they show that simplification opportunities are ignored rather than enforced, which keeps the codebase short of the 90+ band where control flow is uniformly direct.

src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts is a well-typed boundary: executeWclQuery<T>, parseFightRequestBody(body: unknown): FightRequestBody | null, and handleWclFightRequest<TData,TBody> validate runtime shapes before exposing typed data to callers.

src/lib/db/userRecents.ts:160 — getUserRecents<T = unknown>(...): Promise<RecentItem<T>[]> returns `recents as unknown as RecentItem<T>[]`; the generic promises a typed `entityData: T` to callers, but the JSONB column is never validated, so the annotation lies about runtime shape (callers think they hold T, they actually hold unknown).

src/routes/api/top-players/+server.ts:112 uses `new Map<string, any>()` and indexes results with `result[metricKey]` even though the surrounding Drizzle `select({...})` produces a fully inferred row type that would type the metric lookups.

src/components/charts/damageChart.svelte uses 7 `as any` casts (lines 178, 243-265, 547-548) to reach into Chart.js plugin internals — Chart.js has typings, but the chart options bag is hand-typed as `ChartOptions<'line'> & { plugins: { annotation: { annotations: any[] } } }` which loses precision for `annotations`.

src/app.d.ts:21 declares `waitUntil: (promise: Promise<any>) => void` and lines 28-30 contain a real (non-declaration) `export async function post(context) { ... }` with an implicit-any `context` parameter — leftover Cloudflare Workers template noise inside a `.d.ts` file.

src/routes/api/cast-events/+server.ts:63 and src/routes/api/boss-events/+server.ts:74 both bridge GraphQL `Array<{type:string} & Record<string,unknown>>` data into `CastEvent[]` via `as unknown as CastEvent` — pragmatic, but the cast is asserted not validated.

Public APIs in $lib (calculations/, server/, auth/, db/) consistently carry explicit parameter and return types; `unknown` is used uniformly for caught errors and external JSON (logger.ts, clientLog.ts, fights/+server.ts, player-details/+server.ts).

No TypeScript `enum`s exist in the codebase, so the enum-bypass anti-pattern is structurally absent; role/metric/spec lists are typed as `string[]` literals in src/routes/api/top-players/+server.ts, which is a minor missed opportunity for `as const` / union narrowing but not a contradiction with runtime.
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: Boundary-level cast in getUserRecents<T> turns the generic into a runtime fiction; the Map<string, any> in top-players abandons recoverable Drizzle row typing; app.d.ts ships an implicit-any function body in a declaration file.

cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: Layering is genuinely clean: a developer reading the directory profiles and coupling matrix sees a near-leaf graph (auth -> types only; db is independent except via a dead-code edge), no cycles, and consistent route -> lib direction. The friction is concentrated at the db boundary, where the documented two-database split is blurred by db/index.ts's barrel re-exporting user-DB modules and by getRaidDb/getUserDb each inlining the connection-factory contract that connection.ts was created to centralise. A secondary store-root split (src/stores.ts vs $lib/stores/) leaks across the lib boundary via ../../stores imports, but the blast radius is small.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase sits between the 90 anchor ('mostly coherent with isolated boundary drift') and the 80 anchor ('recurring boundary violations or hub modules'). The structural backbone earns the high anchor — only two cross-directory edges, no cycles, leaf-style db/server/auth — but two genuine drifts (documented connection-factory contract not enforced; db barrel mixing two databases against its own header comment) plus the lib/non-lib store split keep it from sitting cleanly at 90. Drift is localised rather than systemic, hence 83.0.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: Top-level shape is recognisable SvelteKit (routes/, lib/, components/, static/) with one genuinely strong domain split — the deliberate two-database separation is documented inside userSchema.ts and mirrored in drizzle configs and migration trees. Underneath that veneer ownership is repeatedly blurred: an empty README leaves no project contract, db/index.ts barrels user-DB modules across the documented database boundary, a 'shared connection factory' module is dead, src/lib/utils.ts and src/lib/utils/ collide, two component roots have no dividing rule, and src/routes/api/ mixes three naming idioms a new engineer cannot predict.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: Evidence is mixed-leaning-negative: the basic decomposition is sensible and one major boundary (raid vs user DB) is well documented, but multiple top-level structural commitments are violated by the code that's supposed to enforce them (db/index.ts, connection.ts) and the project's own entry contract (README) is absent. Per 'when mixed, score lower', a mid-50s score reflects that a new engineer can navigate the tree but cannot trust it to mean what its names and docs imply.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: The codebase is broadly very consistent: uniform camelCase, a small fixed verb vocabulary, named-export modules everywhere, and a deliberate factory pattern in lib/auth that explains its sibling-behavior anomalies. The remaining drift is concentrated in src/routes/api: one WCL endpoint (death-events) bypasses the shared handler its peers use, and DB-backed endpoints expose snake_case response fields while WCL endpoints expose camelCase, with no documented contract.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The dimension sits in the upper-middle band: there are no codebase-wide style islands or naming-convention drifts, but the API route folder has two real, navigable inconsistencies (one orchestration outlier, one response-shape split across ~6 endpoints) that a new contributor would notice within minutes and would have to ask about. That keeps the score below the 'reliably consistent' tier (~85+) but well above codebases with mixed casing or export styles.

error_consistency.dimension_character: The codebase has the right primitives in place — apiError/apiOk for shape, logServerError/handleApiError for server logs, WclQueryError for upstream typing, clientLog for browser code, and a Result-type Network-boundary in src/lib/data — but the helpers are applied unevenly. A developer reading one route handler can predict the shape, then opens a sibling route and finds the same logic spelled out by hand, a different status code for the same upstream failure, or, worst of all in character-reports, a top-level catch that silently converts a thrown error into an empty success payload. The strengths are systemic, but the inconsistencies cluster on the most user-facing seams (API handlers, server-side data modules) where predictability matters most.
error_consistency.score_rationale: This sits between the 80 and 70 anchors: the patterns exist and are documented, which keeps it above 'unpredictable', but a developer regularly encounters mixed strategies in related code paths — handleApiError vs manual pairs, 500 vs 502 for identical WclQueryError, generic-message vs raw-message client responses, swallowed vs logged catches in sibling server modules, console.error vs logServerError on the server. The character-reports catch-as-empty-success and the api/blizzard error-message leak are concrete trust failures, not stylistic drift, which pulls the score below 80; the strong, well-documented primitives keep it from falling near 60.

naming_quality.dimension_character: Names in this codebase are predominantly precise and domain-aligned: verb prefixes (fetch/get/is/has/handle) are applied consistently with their semantic meaning, and module/file names track the rich raid/wcl/raiderio/blizzard vocabulary. The defects are narrow, local closures and a single abbreviation outlier rather than systemic vocabulary drift, so a developer can usually predict behavior from a name without opening the file.
naming_quality.score_rationale: This sits between 90 (strong, names mostly precise with a few that need a second look) and 80 (regularly encountering generic verbs / mismatches). Three confirmed local issues — doSave, res, and the hide closure — are real but each is isolated to a single function and surrounded by otherwise clean naming. The consistency of fetch*/get*/is*/handle* across many files and the absence of generic process/manage verbs in production code keep this in the upper half of the 80s rather than dropping into the 80-anchor band.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: Abstractions in this codebase are mostly earned: the WCL request factory, token-cache factory, requireSession helper, and dbOperation each remove real duplication or standardise a behaviour, and there is no one-implementation interface sprawl or facade re-export entropy. The remaining cost is small but visible — one sibling route opts out of the established factory and two auth modules expose dead pass-through re-exports — which keeps the pattern from feeling uniformly trusted.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Solidly in the 80s: a developer benefits from most layers and rarely navigates ceremony. It is not 90+ because handleWclFightRequest is the established pattern across four siblings yet death-events still inlines the same plumbing, and isTokenExpired is re-exported from two auth modules with zero callers — both are concrete instances of indirection that does not pay for itself.

dependency_health.dimension_character: The runtime dependency set is small (12 packages), single-vendor per concern (Auth.js for auth, Drizzle for DB, Chart.js family for charts, Zod + Superforms + Formsnap for forms), and every entry is actually imported by src/. Standard shadcn pairings (clsx + tailwind-merge) are complementary rather than duplicative, and the optional chartjs-plugin-zoom is lazy-loaded — showing weight awareness. There are no observed unused runtime deps, no competing libraries for the same purpose, and no top-level version conflicts in the lockfile.
dependency_health.score_rationale: A developer encounters a cohesive, purposeful dependency set with no duplication or evident bloat, which sits between the 90 (mostly healthy with minor overlap or weight concerns) and 100 (exemplary) anchors. The chart stack is the heaviest cluster but is justified by direct chart usage and is partially deferred via dynamic import. A small notch below 100 reflects that this review is bounded to declared deps without a bundle-size audit, not any concrete drift signal.

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: Function and class internals are mostly tight: shared wrappers (dbOperation, handleWclFightRequest, apiOk/apiError, fetchWithTimeout) give related modules a uniform local shape, and discriminated-union return types keep error/empty/ok branches explicit instead of nullable-everywhere. The rough edges are concentrated in a few specific endpoints — top-players has duplicated select() blocks, dead diagnostic code, and an `any`-typed dedup loop, while character-reports embeds a fallback loop over two literally-identical GraphQL queries — but they are isolated, not the prevailing style.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Most internals read at the 90 anchor (clean craft with isolated rough edges), but two endpoints land closer to 80 (regularly-encountered local complexity for anyone touching them) and a few smaller files carry minor `let`-for-`const` and inline-construction noise, so the holistic score sits between the two anchors. Strong shared helpers and consistent extraction balance keep this above 80; the concrete defects in top-players and character-reports keep it well below 90.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: The seam library is mostly the right one: a tight set of helpers (apiError/apiOk, requireSession, logServerError/handleApiError, executeWclQuery, handleWclFightRequest, createTokenCache, WclQueryError) capture the recurring boundary patterns and most route handlers compose them well. The friction is that a few collaborators don't plug into the seams that exist for them — death-events re-implements the WCL fight orchestration inline, both DB wrappers ignore createDrizzlePostgres, the auth↔locals seam is plumbed over HTTP, and DB-backed handlers each own an ad-hoc row-to-DTO translator instead of a shared mapper. A developer making a cross-module change will find the happy path obvious but will hit one of these bespoke seams often enough that 'I followed the pattern' isn't a reliable strategy.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: Above 60 (significant drag) because most boundaries are explicit, factored, and type-safe; below 90 because the wins are undermined by a small number of high-traffic seams (DB construction, session reading, fight-request orchestration, body validation) that each have a working factory the codebase declines to use uniformly. The remaining glue-code entropy in DB→DTO mapping is systemic across ~8 handlers and prevents a confident higher score.

test_strategy.dimension_character: The test portfolio is small (11 files), narrowly scoped to a few utility helpers, and structurally inverted relative to risk: the core scoring engine, every API handler, all OAuth/token-cache code, every server load function, hooks.server.ts and the stores layer have zero coverage, while a meaningful share of the existing tests are tautologies (config-literal restatements, barrel re-exports, `typeof === 'function'`) or copy/class-coupled component snapshots that will churn without catching real defects. A developer changing rating math, an API handler, an auth flow, or a server load function gets no safety net from this suite, and a developer changing marketing copy or a Tailwind utility will see unrelated tests fail.
test_strategy.score_rationale: Per the dimension anchor, 60 is 'significant drag' and 40 is 'poor — actively works against the developer'; this codebase sits closer to the 'poor' end because the riskiest paths (scoring math, 22 API handlers, auth) are entirely unvalidated while the suite simultaneously generates noise via tautology tests, a timing-fragile performance.test.ts, and copy-coupled component tests. The handful of well-written unit tests (abilityMetadata, warcraftlogs, interactionUtils, utils) and the absence of snapshot overuse keep the score from collapsing further, but the strategy is structurally inadequate for confident change.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The API surface has strong shared scaffolding — uniform `{error}` envelope via apiOk/apiError, a single auth helper (requireSession), and a shared per-fight WCL pipeline (handleWclFightRequest + parseFightRequestBody) that gives the WCL family genuinely predictable shape. The drag is recurring shape inconsistency across the broader surface: response field naming is split between snake_case (DB-derived endpoints) and camelCase (everything else) with mixed conventions even within a single endpoint, body-validation rigor swings between strict type guards and unchecked destructuring, sibling external proxies handle errors differently, and one route (character-score) actively breaks the error envelope by returning the success shape on a 400. A developer who learns one corner of the API cannot reliably predict the next.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The shared helpers and consistent async/auth posture pull the score above the 'mixed' anchor, but the snake_vs_camel response split, the 400-with-success-shape regression in character-score, and the inconsistent body validation across POST endpoints are exactly the 'recurring irregularities' described at the 80 anchor. Conservatively scored at 78.0 because the shape drift is systemic across many endpoints rather than isolated, and one defect (character-score 400) breaks a contract the rest of the codebase relies on.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: Authorization in this codebase is small, simple, and applied through a single chokepoint: requireSession is the only auth primitive, every protected API handler uses it identically, and user identity for writes is always pulled from the session rather than the request body. The model is single-bit (signed-in vs not — no roles, no RLS, no service-role bypass) which removes whole categories of inconsistency the rubric warns about; the only real unevenness is at the input-validation half of the trust boundary, where authenticated JSON-body POSTs skip the parse-error handling and shape checks their public siblings apply.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: A developer auditing protected routes would find auth coverage trustworthy — one helper, one error shape, one identity source — which sits above the 80 'recurring gaps' anchor and into the 90 'mostly consistent with limited exceptions' band. The input-validation asymmetry at authenticated POST endpoints is a recurring, observable inconsistency at the same trust boundary, which keeps the score off the 90 anchor and lands it at 87.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase actively fights LLM-style debt: a single handleWclFightRequest helper collapses what would otherwise be 12+ near-identical fetch/parse/try-catch route handlers, a small logger module pins log shape, and JSDoc consistently explains WHY (deployment split, scope changes, fallback chains) rather than echoing signatures. Residual debt is concentrated in one client page (object-dump console.error and a few restating one-line comments) and a stray debug:true in auth.ts; nothing systemic.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: Per the global anchors this sits in the upper-80s band — 'mostly clean with small pockets of boilerplate or restating patterns' fits exactly. The deliberate consolidation helpers are stronger than 80-tier code, but the raid logs page object dump, a handful of restating comments, and the always-on auth debug flag keep it short of 90.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The codebase has just completed an ambitious multi-phase major-version migration (Svelte 4→5, Vite 5→7, Tailwind 3→4, bits-ui 0→2, formsnap 1→2, Zod 3→4, superforms unpinned), and runtime evidence shows the new ecosystem is the dominant idiom — runes, snippets, Svelte 5 component shapes, and the modern dep set are pervasive. The residue is small, localized, and explicitly tracked: a handful of `svelte/legacy` `run()` shims left by the codemod and a deferred-by-design `$app/stores` → `$app/state` follow-up that MIGRATION_PLAN.md calls out by name. A developer encounters mostly-complete migration with two narrow coexistence pockets and one stale comment in db/schema.ts.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: Per the rubric, 100 requires migrations to be complete or intentionally bounded with clear documentation, and 90 allows minor legacy residue that doesn't cause confusion; 80 is when old-and-new patterns visibly coexist and create 'which do I follow?' friction. This codebase sits between 90 and 80: the residue is small in absolute terms but the `svelte/legacy run()` calls inside otherwise rune-native components, plus the still-pervasive `$app/stores` imports, do create momentary inconsistency for a contributor. 85 reflects 'mostly complete, two clearly-bounded pockets remain, and they're documented as deliberate follow-ups rather than active drift'.

package_organization.dimension_character: The repository follows SvelteKit conventions cleanly at the route layer and has several well-scoped, cohesive packages under src/lib/ (auth/, server/, data/, types/, components/ui/), so most navigation is predictable. However, the lib/ root itself shows recurring placement friction: a utils.ts file collides in name with a utils/ folder, stores live in two places, single-file folders sit next to populated ones, and the db/ directory flattens two distinct databases into one bag of files. A developer can navigate the framework parts confidently but has to read each file under src/ and src/lib/ to know which db, store, or util module is the right one.
package_organization.score_rationale: The dimension sits between the 80 and 70 anchors: cohesive packages and consistent route structure pull it toward 'solid but uneven', while the utils name collision, dual store homes, fragmented single-file folders, and the unsplit dual-database db/ are the kind of structural mismatches the 80 anchor describes ('files that don't belong where they are, flat directories mixing unrelated concerns'). With multiple recurring mismatches but no widespread search-rather-than-navigate problem, 76.0 reflects a codebase that mostly serves navigation but routinely surprises within src/lib/.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: Initialization is deliberately lazy and isolated: every database client, OAuth token, and external-credential read is hidden behind a factory or closure that runs at call time, not at module load. The few module-level singletons that exist (token caches, the roster Map) are documented per-isolate decisions with clear concurrency notes, and a `building` guard prevents accidental connections during prerender.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: A developer can import any module in this codebase without worrying about boot-order side effects, environment timing, or hidden cross-module init coupling — matching the 90 anchor. One minor `process.env.NODE_ENV` outlier and the unavoidable framework-level SvelteKitAuth bootstrap keep it from a higher score, but there is no fragility, no circular-init shimming, and no creation-order dependency between modules.

design_coherence.dimension_character: The codebase shows mostly coherent design: route handlers delegate to focused server modules, the WCL event endpoints share a single handleWclFightRequest helper, dbOperation wraps DB access, and apiOk/apiError/logServerError are used uniformly. Defects are isolated but real — duplicated postgres connection config across the two DB factories with a dead connection.ts factory sitting next to it, and a couple of fat route handlers (top-players, character-reports) that bundle parsing, query construction, dedup, and ranking in one body.
design_coherence.score_rationale: Pulled toward the 80 anchor by consistent abstraction patterns (handleWclFightRequest, dbOperation, scoreSources resolver, page.server.ts thinness). Pulled below by the ~30-line db-client duplication that should consolidate into the existing connection.ts factory and by two route files that mix several distinct responsibilities. The mechanical 'orphaned' flags on SvelteKit/vitest entry points are framework false positives and don't depress quality further.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: Contract discipline is unusually deliberate for a SvelteKit app: there's a documented dbOperation invariant honored across every db/*.ts module, discriminated-union return types (FetchRunsResult, AddOutcome, ScoreLookup, requireSession's `{session}|{response}`) used consistently at every async boundary, and explicit JSDoc on cross-module helpers (tokenCacheBase, requireSession, scoreSources, myWowRoster) that accurately describes throw/return behavior. The defects are isolated: a couple of `@throws Never` claims that miss a JSON-parse path, one persistence side effect documented as part of the contract but silently swallowed, and a partial-and-unused db barrel. A developer would generally trust the signatures and docs, but would occasionally find that 'never throws' means 'mostly never throws' and that a 'persists then returns' helper sometimes only does the latter.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Closer to the 80 anchor (solid but uneven) than 90: the codebase clearly invests in contract clarity — discriminated unions, named errors (WclQueryError), shared dbOperation pattern, explicit @throws documentation — and that effort holds across most of src/lib. The score is pulled down by a small number of doc/behavior divergences in characterData.ts and bnetAccount.ts and a misleading barrel in db/index.ts; pulled up by the rarity of finding deliberate `@throws Never` annotations and consistent error-shape contracts at all. Scoring conservatively at 80 because the inaccurate @throws claims sit on the public data layer used by every character flow.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: Across the 104 files of this small SvelteKit app, control flow is overwhelmingly direct: guard-clause early returns, awaited promise chains, drizzle query builders, and predictable try/catch around I/O are the norm and a developer can almost always trace a route handler from request to response without surprises. The exceptions are isolated rather than systemic — one genuinely broken fallback loop (two identical GraphQL queries iterated as if they were alternates), a placeholder-comment dead branch in top-players, an always-undefined SEO field, and a mild stylistic habit of marking purely-synchronous SvelteKit handlers `async`. None of these obscure the intent of the surrounding module, but they are the kind of small clarity leaks that a reader has to stop and reason about.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase sits between the 90 anchor (mostly clear with isolated simplification opportunities) and the 80 anchor (regularly encounters redundant branches/dead paths). The dead-loop in character-reports and the empty-if in top-players are the kind of defect the 90 anchor explicitly forbids in a uniform sense, but they are concentrated in a couple of files rather than being a recurring shape — the rest of the routes and lib/ helpers read cleanly. Per the 'when mixed, score lower' guidance and the presence of 4-5 genuine local issues, I land just below the 'mostly clear' band.

type_safety.dimension_character: TypeScript is strict, `any` in production code is rare (a handful of sites), and external data is consistently funnelled through `unknown`-typed boundary helpers (parseFightRequestBody, executeWclQuery, logger error params) before being narrowed — a developer can generally trust signatures as documentation. The soft spots are concentrated and shape-recognisable: a generic `getUserRecents<T>` whose body lies about its return shape, a Map<string, any> that throws away a recoverable Drizzle row type, and a Chart.js plugin options bag that escapes into `any` to reach plugin internals.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase comfortably clears the 80-anchor (strict mode, well-typed cores, narrow boundaries) but a developer would still hit two recurring frictions: a generic return-type that isn't enforced (userRecents) and a few targeted any-escapes inside otherwise typed modules (top-players Map, damageChart Chart.js plugin, app.d.ts leftover). That keeps it in the 'generally accurate with a few soft spots that don't cause real confusion' band rather than at the 90+ trust-the-types level.

issues.summary: src/lib/db/index.ts barrel re-exports user-DB modules, eroding the documented two-database boundary at the import surface
issues.suggestion: Make src/lib/db/index.ts the raid-DB entry only — export getRaidDb plus a curated re-export of schema.ts (raid tables) — and remove the user-DB re-exports. Either keep the existing direct imports for user-DB modules, or add a sibling barrel `src/lib/db/user/index.ts` that re-exports getUserDb + userSchema + users + userRecents and migrate the four user-DB consumers to it. Either way restores the documented two-database boundary at the import surface.
evidence: src/lib/db/index.ts ends with `export * from './userSchema'; export * from './users'; export * from './userRecents'; export { getUserDb } from './userDb'`, so `$lib/db` is both the raid-DB factory entry and a user-DB barrel.

src/lib/db/schema.ts module doc explicitly defines two intentionally separate databases (DATABASE_URL raid analytics vs DATABASE_USER_URL user/auth) and points at userSchema.ts for the user side, contradicting the unified barrel.

Real user-DB consumers do not use the barrel: api/character-reports, api/current-state, api/recent-reports, api/recent-characters all import directly from `$lib/db/userRecents.js`; data/myWowRoster.ts imports directly from `$lib/db/bnetAccount` and `$lib/db/userCharacters`; auth.ts imports `$lib/db/userDb.js`, `$lib/db/userSchema.js`, `$lib/db/users.js`. The user-DB half of the barrel has no active external need.

issues.summary: Documented single-factory architecture in src/lib/db/connection.ts is not enforced — getRaidDb and getUserDb each inline the full client config
issues.suggestion: Either adopt the factory (rewrite getRaidDb in db/index.ts to `return createDrizzlePostgres({ label: 'raid', connectionString: env.DATABASE_URL, connectionStringSource: 'DATABASE_URL' })` and getUserDb similarly with DATABASE_USER_URL || DATABASE_URL), or delete connection.ts and update its module doc claim. Adopting is preferred — the factory already owns logServerError-based error reporting, which would also remove the lone `src/lib/db/ -> src/lib/server/` cross-directory edge once the dead file is removed from the import graph.
evidence: src/lib/db/connection.ts module doc states 'Both databases ... connect with identical client settings ... This module owns those settings so the two getXxxDb() wrappers ... differ only by the env var they read and the scope tag used for logging.' and exports createDrizzlePostgres for that purpose.

src/lib/db/index.ts getRaidDb (lines ~7-50) and src/lib/db/userDb.ts getUserDb (lines ~7-52) each call postgres() directly with the same prepare/max/idle_timeout/connect_timeout/transform/types/onnotice/debug/fetch_types/max_lifetime block instead of calling createDrizzlePostgres.

createDrizzlePostgres has zero importers in the workspace, making connection.ts a documented contract that runtime ignores — exactly the 'documented architecture contracts drifting from runtime' look-for of this dimension.

issues.summary: src/stores.ts at the top-level src/ duplicates the role of $lib/stores/, forcing components to cross the lib boundary via ../../stores imports
issues.suggestion: Move src/stores.ts contents into src/lib/stores/ — e.g. a new src/lib/stores/calculatorState.ts (or split into apiPopup.ts/dungeonData.ts/wowSummary.ts to match the recentCharacters/recentReports granularity) — and update apiForm.svelte and keystoneTable.svelte to `import { ... } from '$lib/stores/calculatorState'`. This collapses the two store roots into one, removes the lib-crossing relative import, and aligns calculator state with the existing stores convention.
evidence: src/stores.ts (writable apiPopup, dungeonData, wowSummaryStore) lives at the top-level src/ root, while src/lib/stores/ already hosts recentCharacters/recentReports plus their backends — two store roots with no documented dividing line.

Components reach the top-level file with two-level relative imports that cross the lib boundary: apiForm.svelte line 11 `import { apiPopup, dungeonData, wowSummaryStore } from '../../stores';`, keystoneTable.svelte lines 17-19 same pattern. No other production module imports from `../../` past src/ except these two.

structure.directory_profiles for src/ shows it as a mixed directory of unrelated concerns (auth.ts, hooks.server.ts, stores.ts, test-setup.ts, test-utils.ts) — the only one of those five that is non-entry-point production code is stores.ts.

issues.summary: README.md is empty — repo has no top-level contract for a new engineer.
issues.suggestion: Write a short README that names the product, lists the two databases (raid analytics vs user/auth) and their env vars, points at PRODUCT.md/DESIGN.md, and documents the routes/api naming rule and the two component roots so the directory tree can be read at face value.
evidence: README.md is 0 bytes.
PRODUCT.md/DESIGN.md exist but neither is the conventional first-read entry, and nothing in the repo points to them as a substitute.

issues.summary: src/lib/db/index.ts barrel-exports user-DB modules, conflating the documented two-database split.
issues.suggestion: Stop re-exporting user-DB symbols from the raid-DB index. Either keep `$lib/db` as a raid-only entry and require user-DB callers to import from `$lib/db/userDb` / `$lib/db/userSchema` directly, or split into `$lib/db/raid/` and `$lib/db/user/` so the boundary the docstring promises is visible at the import site.

issues.summary: src/lib/db/connection.ts factory is unused; getRaidDb and getUserDb still inline the same options.

issues.summary: src/components/ and src/lib/components/ui/ are two component roots with no documented dividing rule.
issues.suggestion: Document the rule (e.g. 'src/lib/components/ui/ = generic primitives only; src/components/ = app-specific feature components') in README/DESIGN.md, or move src/components/ under src/lib/components/<feature>/ so the single root reflects the convention. Either choice should be visible to a new engineer without code-reading.
evidence: src/components/ contains feature components (calculator/, charts/, raid/, layout/, seo.svelte, authButton.svelte).

issues.summary: death-events POST hand-rolls the WCL request lifecycle while every sibling WCL endpoint delegates to handleWclFightRequest.

issues.summary: DB-backed /api endpoints emit snake_case response fields while WCL-backed /api endpoints emit camelCase, with no documented contract.

issues.summary: character-reports outer catch returns apiOk({reports:[],guilds:[]}), making thrown errors indistinguishable from a real empty result
issues.suggestion: Replace the outer catch body with `return handleApiError('api/character-reports', error)` so failures become a 500 with the standard generic message. If a partial-degradation UX is wanted, return apiOk with an explicit `{ reports, guilds, degraded: true }` flag rather than overloading the empty-success shape.
evidence: Line 299-302: `} catch (error) { logServerError('api/character-reports', 'request failed', error); return apiOk({ reports: [], guilds: [] } satisfies CharacterReportsResponse); }`
The same-shape empty payload is also a legitimate success branch (line 218 when merged.length === 0), so clients cannot distinguish 'user has no roster' from 'WCL/DB blew up'.
Contradicts the documented dbOperation/handleApiError contract used by every other handler that fails-loud with apiError(...).

issues.summary: Half of the API handlers inline `logServerError(...) + apiError(...)` instead of the documented handleApiError helper, creating two parallel idioms with identical output
issues.suggestion: Migrate every `logServerError(scope, ..., error); return apiError(...)` pair in a route handler's outer catch to `return handleApiError(scope, error, optionalClientMessage, optionalStatus)`. The only handlers that should keep manual pairs are ones that log additional context BEFORE the response (e.g. fights' WclQueryError branch already needs custom handling) — and those should use handleApiError for the fallthrough.
evidence: src/lib/server/logger.ts:30-49 documents handleApiError as the catch-block helper with an example: `} catch (error) { return handleApiError('api/foo', error); }`.

browse-logs, top-players, damage-average, spec-top-players, death-hotspots, spec-statistics use handleApiError. current-state (lines 39, 90), recent-characters (30, 62), player-details (42), fights (63), death-events (104), character-reports (300), blizzard (46) inline the helper's body by hand.
Both branches produce the same `{ error: string }` shape and the same '[scope] request failed' log line — pure pattern drift with no semantic difference.

issues.summary: WCL upstream failures return HTTP 502 via handleWclFightRequest but 500 from sibling handlers that inline their own try/catch around the same WclQueryError
issues.suggestion: Either migrate fights/death-events/character-reports onto handleWclFightRequest (the body-shape mismatch in fights/character-reports may require a sibling helper for non-fight queries), or in the inline catch branches change `apiError('Failed to fetch ... from API.')` to `apiError('Failed to fetch ... from API.', 502)` so WclQueryError ⇒ 502 everywhere.
evidence: src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts:117 — `if (error instanceof WclQueryError) { return apiError(options.fetchErrorMessage, 502); }`.
src/routes/api/fights/+server.ts:58-60 — `if (error instanceof WclQueryError) { logServerError(...); return apiError('Failed to fetch fights from API.'); }` — defaults to 500.
src/routes/api/death-events/+server.ts:100-102 — same shape, also defaults to 500.
Same failure class (upstream WCL transport/GraphQL error) ⇒ two different HTTP statuses depending on which file the route lives in.

issues.summary: api/blizzard returns the raw error.message to the client, leaking upstream Blizzard payload bodies and diverging from sibling handlers' generic messages
issues.suggestion: Replace the catch body with `return handleApiError('api/blizzard', error);` so the upstream body stays in server logs (via logServerError) and the client gets the standard generic message. If a more specific user-facing string is wanted, pass it as the third argument: `handleApiError('api/blizzard', error, 'Failed to fetch character profile.')`.
evidence: Line 32-36: `if (!summaryResponse.ok) { throw new Error(`Summary endpoint error: ${await summaryResponse.text()}`); }` — the upstream HTTP body is embedded in the thrown message.
Line 47: `return apiError(error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Internal Server Error.');` — that message is sent verbatim to the client.
Every sibling handler (top-players, recent-reports, fights, etc.) returns a generic 'Failed to fetch ...' or 'Internal Server Error.' string.

issues.summary: Server-side modules outside src/lib/server/ and src/lib/db/ use bare console.error and empty catches, bypassing logServerError and breaking the documented log shape
issues.suggestion: Replace each bare console.error in myWowRoster.ts and bnetAccount.ts with `logServerError('myWowRoster', '<what failed>', error)` (or `logServerError('db/bnetAccount', ..., error)`). For the empty catch in bnetAccount.ts:99-101, at least call `logServerError('db/bnetAccount', 'token-row update failed (best-effort)', error)` so persistent persistence failures become visible — the comment is right that we don't want to throw, but silent is the wrong default.
evidence: src/lib/data/myWowRoster.ts:95,160,214 — `console.error('myWowRoster: region ${region} failed', error)` and similar; the file's own JSDoc admits errors are 'surfaced... via console.error'.
src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts:68 — `console.error('battlenet: refresh fetch failed')` — no error object passed at all.
src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts:73 — `console.error('battlenet: refresh rejected', response.status)` — passes status but not the response body or any structured object.
src/lib/db/bnetAccount.ts:99-101 — empty `} catch { /* Ignore persistence failures here; callers can still use the fresh token. */ }` — server-side DB write errors disappear with no log at all.
logger.ts's '[scope] message' format means a `grep '\[battlenet\]'` search will miss every one of these failures.

issues.summary: Client-side store backends use raw console.error strings instead of the existing logClientError helper, leaving the client log shape inconsistent with the documented server one
issues.suggestion: Replace each console.error in src/lib/stores/recentCharactersBackends.ts and recentReportsBackends.ts with `logClientError('recentCharacters/api', '<what failed>', error)` (or scope='recentReports/localStorage' etc.). Pick stable scope tags so '[recentCharacters' grep covers the whole feature on both sides of the seam.

Result: client and server logs both carry '[scope] message' shape in some files but free-form prose in others — a developer cannot grep one prefix and find every failure for a feature.

issues.summary: doSave inner closure in saveToAPI uses the generic 'do' prefix and adds no semantic information beyond its enclosing function name

issues.summary: deathHotspots.svelte uses `res` for a fetch response while every other fetch site in the codebase uses `response`

issues.summary: Local `hide` closure in hideWowheadTooltips bundles API + DOM clears and runs three scheduled passes; the bare name obscures the multi-step retry intent

The enclosing exported function is already named `hideWowheadTooltips`; the inner `hide` is a bare verb that shadows the outer concept without disambiguating it.

issues.summary: death-events/+server.ts duplicates handleWclFightRequest plumbing instead of using the shared factory the four sibling event routes adopt.

issues.summary: src/routes/api/top-players/+server.ts duplicates a 10-column select block, keeps unused diagnostic state, has a dead empty branch, and uses `any` in a per-request dedup loop
issues.suggestion: Extract a single buildQuery() that returns the typed select() and apply `.limit(role === 'dps' ? 2000 : Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER)` (or call .limit conditionally on the same builder). Drop `duplicatesFound` and the empty `if (results.length > 0)` block. Type the dedup map with the inferred row type (`type Row = Awaited<typeof query>[number]; const playerMap = new Map<string, Row>()`). Precompute `HEALER_SPECS_LC = new Set(HEALER_SPECS.map(s => s.toLowerCase()))` etc. and test with `Set.has(specName.toLowerCase())`.
