Lines ~131-149: HEALER_SPECS / TANK_SPECS membership tests use `.some(spec => specName.toLowerCase() === spec.toLowerCase())` per row instead of a precomputed lowercased Set.

issues.summary: fetchReportsForGuild loops over two GraphQL query strings that are byte-identical and short-circuits on the first catch, so the apparent 'try second query on failure' fallback never runs
issues.suggestion: Either delete one of the duplicate constants and replace the loop with a single try/catch around `executeWclQuery`, or — if a real fallback was intended — make the second query meaningfully different and move the `return []` out of the catch so iteration continues on failure (`continue` instead of `return`).
evidence: Lines ~67-104: GUILD_RECENT_REPORTS_QUERY and GUILD_REPORTS_QUERY have the same operation arguments (limit: 8) and identical selection sets.
Lines ~129-145: `for (const query of queries) { try { ... } catch { ...; return []; } }` — the catch returns immediately, so iteration never reaches the second query even if it differed.
Net effect: a reader sees a fallback pattern that is structurally a no-op — the loop runs at most one effective iteration.

issues.summary: src/routes/api/browse-logs/+server.ts uses `let` for arrays/builders that are never reassigned, mixing real reassignment with declaration noise
issues.suggestion: Type whereConditions as `const whereConditions: SQL[] = []` (matching the pattern already used in top-players/+server.ts), make `query` const, and replace the conditional reassignment of filteredLogsData with a const ternary. Pure local cleanup, no behavior change.
evidence: Line 14: `let whereConditions = [];` — only `.push()`d, never reassigned, and untyped (any[]).
Line 28: `let query = getRaidDb().select({...})...orderBy(...)` — never reassigned.

Line 47: `let filteredLogsData = compositionsData.map(...)` — only conditionally reassigned via `.filter` later, which would read more clearly as `const baseLogs = ...; const filteredLogsData = params.healerSpecs?.length ? baseLogs.filter(...) : baseLogs;`.

issues.summary: handleSession in src/hooks.server.ts wraps a network call without a try/catch and rebuilds the CSP array every request
issues.suggestion: Wrap the fetch in `try { ... } catch { return null; }` to match the existing 'unavailable → null' contract that consumers already rely on. Hoist the CSP string to a module constant. (The bigger architectural concern — self-fetching /auth/session instead of calling auth() — is tracked under mid_level_elegance and is out of scope here.)
evidence: Lines 40-49: `await fetch(\`${event.url.origin}/auth/session\`, ...)` is unwrapped — a fetch rejection (DNS, abort, fetch implementation throwing) propagates out of getSession() and into every consumer that calls it, instead of being normalised to `null` like the !response.ok branch already does.
Lines 21-32: the 11-entry CSP array is rebuilt and joined on every request even though it is fully static; it could be a module-level `const CSP = [...].join('; ')`.

issues.summary: Every DB-backed +server.ts owns its own row→snake_case API mapper; no shared boundary translator
issues.suggestion: Introduce per-table row→DTO mappers in $lib/server (e.g. mapSpecPerformanceRow, mapDamageAverageRow, mapHealerCompositionRow) that return the exact public shape and live next to their schema/types module. Routes call `data.map(mapSpecPerformanceRow)` and own only filtering/orchestration. This is the same factor-out move that handleWclFightRequest already proved out for the WCL side.
evidence: damage-average/+server.ts selects `time_seconds: damageAverages.timeSeconds` and then re-maps `time_seconds → time_seconds, avg_damage → avg, std_dev → std, count → n, confidence_interval → ci` inline.
spec-statistics/+server.ts selects 22 columns aliased to snake_case, with no shared mapper for the public API contract.

spec-top-players/+server.ts maps `r.player_name → player_name`, `dps/hps → value`, `report_code+fight_id → report_url` inline; top-players/+server.ts duplicates the same shape with only the `role`/`metric` switch differing.
browse-logs/+server.ts manually re-keys camelCase Drizzle output back to snake_case for the wire format inside the handler body.
Result: the DB-naming-convention boundary leaks into 8+ route files and there is no single place to change the public DTO shape when the schema changes.

issues.summary: raiderio/+server.ts is the only external-fetch route without the standard try/catch + logServerError seam
issues.suggestion: Wrap the body in `try { ... } catch (error) { return handleApiError('api/raiderio', error, 'Failed to fetch data'); }` so this route honors the same boundary contract as blizzard/character-reports. Same fix surface as one edit.
evidence: raiderio/+server.ts calls `await fetch(apiUrl)` and `await response.json()` with no try/catch; a network throw bubbles unhandled and the route returns an unstructured 500 with no `[api/raiderio] ...` log line.

Sibling external-fetch handlers (blizzard/+server.ts at line 21–47, character-reports/+server.ts at line 169–301) all wrap external IO in `try { ... } catch (error) { logServerError('api/<scope>', 'request failed', error); return apiError(...) }`.
The codebase has a documented logger seam (`logServerError`/`handleApiError`) intended to give every boundary failure a uniform `[scope]` line; raiderio silently opts out.

issues.summary: Catch-block error translation alternates between handleApiError() and logServerError()+apiError() with no rule
issues.suggestion: Pick one form (handleApiError is shorter and is what the doc recommends) and migrate the remaining ~8 routes. When a route needs a non-default status (e.g. 502 for upstream failures), extend handleApiError with an explicit status arg rather than reverting to the two-line form.
evidence: handleApiError(scope, error, msg) is used by browse-logs, damage-average, death-hotspots, spec-statistics, spec-top-players, top-players.
logServerError(scope, ..., error); return apiError(msg) is used by current-state, recent-reports, recent-characters, blizzard, fights, death-events, player-details, character-reports.
Both forms produce the identical log line and identical 500 response (handleApiError simply calls logServerError + apiError). The handler reader has to recognize two shapes for the same intent.
The logger module's docstring on handleApiError already markets it as the canonical form ('Use at every top-level } catch (error) { in a route handler'), but only ~6 of ~14 catch blocks adopted it.

issues.summary: src/hooks.server.ts (CSP + Auth.js handle) has no tests despite running on every request
issues.suggestion: Add a small unit test that invokes the exported `handle` with a fake `RequestEvent` and `resolve` stub, asserting the response carries the expected Content-Security-Policy header and that the Auth.js handle is composed in the documented order. Reuse the same RequestEvent fixture for future +server.ts tests so this also seeds HTTP-handler test infrastructure.
evidence: hooks.server.ts composes the CSP header (connect-src 'self' https:, etc.) and forwards cookies into the Auth.js handle for every server-rendered request and every API call.
No test under src/lib/__tests__ or src/components/__tests__ imports hooks.server.ts; a CSP regression or auth-handle ordering change would ship undetected.
There is no test wrapper (e.g. resolve-mock + RequestEvent fixture) that future hooks tests could plug into.

issues.summary: No test harness exists for SvelteKit +server.ts handlers (no msw, no RequestEvent helper), making the 22-handler coverage gap self-reinforcing
issues.suggestion: Add a thin helper in src/test-utils.ts that builds a minimal RequestEvent (request, url, params, locals, fetch) and exports a `callHandler(handler, init)` wrapper returning the parsed Response. Pair it with msw (or a manual fetch stub) for upstream Blizzard / Warcraft Logs / Raider.io calls. With that harness in place, each +server.ts test becomes ~10 lines and the 22-handler gap can be closed incrementally rather than all-or-nothing.
evidence: src/test-utils.ts and src/test-setup.ts contain no helpers for building a SvelteKit RequestEvent, no fetch mock, and no msw setup.
package.json declares no msw / nock / supertest-style dependency for HTTP-handler testing.

Several handlers (browse-logs, current-state, recent-characters, recent-reports) consume `await request.json()` and cast directly to typed shapes — there is no shared way to write a single test that POSTs a malformed body and asserts a 400.
The lack of harness is why nine prior 'add tests for X handler' issues are individually expensive and have all stayed open.

issues.summary: performance.test.ts asserts `process.memoryUsage().heapUsed` growth < 50MB and depends on optional `global.gc`
issues.suggestion: Delete src/lib/__tests__/performance.test.ts. If a real performance regression suite is wanted later, write benchmarks against actual hot paths (e.g. keystoneCalculations.scoreFormula, abilityMetadata.buildBossAbilityGroups) using vitest's bench API, and report timings rather than gating CI on absolute thresholds.
evidence: The 'should not cause memory leaks with large datasets' test gates on `heapGrowth < 50 * 1024 * 1024` after creating 10× 1000-row arrays, with `if (global.gc) global.gc()` — but vitest is not run with `--expose-gc`, so the GC call is a no-op and heap growth depends on V8's collection timing.
The same file also asserts `parallelTime < sequentialTime / 2` with 20ms setTimeouts, which is sensitive to event-loop jitter on shared CI runners.
Both assertions test fabricated in-test code, not any production module — a failure tells the developer nothing actionable about Mr. Mythical.

issues.summary: API response field naming is split between snake_case (DB endpoints) and camelCase (others) with no documented rule
issues.suggestion: Pick one casing rule (camelCase is most consistent with the existing TypeScript types in src/lib/types/apiTypes.ts and with the calculate-runs/character-score/character-reports endpoints) and apply it to the DB-fed endpoints by remapping in the existing `.map((r) => ({...}))` blocks already present in damage-average, death-hotspots, top-players, spec-top-players, browse-logs, and spec-statistics. Update any frontend consumers in src/components and src/lib/calculations in the same change. No new abstraction needed — the mappers are already there.
evidence: damage-average emits {time_seconds, avg, std, n, ci, encounter_id}; death-hotspots emits {time_seconds, death_count, sample_count}; spec-top-players/top-players emit {player_name, spec_icon, damage_done, healing_done, report_url, report_code, fight_id, is_kill, death_count}; browse-logs emits {report_code, fight_id, encounter_name, healer_specs, ...}.

calculate-runs emits {targetScore, totalScore, breakdown}; character-score emits {score, color, source}; boss-events emits {castEvents, abilities}; recent-characters emits {characterName, realm, region}; character-reports emits {code, title, timestamp, sourceCharacter}.

Within top-players the query string is camelCase (`encounterId`, `fightFilter`, `metric`) while the response body is snake_case (`player_name`, `spec_icon`, `report_url`) — caller has to translate cases at the boundary.
There is no comment or doc anywhere in src/routes/api or src/lib/types/apiTypes.ts explaining when each style is used.

issues.summary: /api/character-score returns the success body shape with HTTP 400, breaking the codebase-wide `{error:string}` error contract
issues.suggestion: Replace the 400 branch with `return apiError('Missing or invalid name/region/realm', 400);` to match the rest of the surface. Update the docblock that promises `400 ScoreResult` to instead document the standard `{error}` envelope.
evidence: src/routes/api/character-score/+server.ts line 38-40: `if (!name || !region || !realm || !VALID_REGIONS.has(region)) { return apiOk({ score: null, color: null, source: null } satisfies ScoreResult, 400); }` — uses `apiOk` with a 400 status and the success-shape body.

Every other endpoint in src/routes/api uses `apiError('...', 400)` for missing/invalid params (e.g. blizzard:14, calculate-runs:21-23, damage-average:14, death-hotspots:13, fights:43, browse-logs/various, spec-statistics:17, top-players:18-22), which produces `{error: string}` per src/lib/server/apiResponses.ts:7-9.
A client doing `const data = await res.json(); if (data.error) ...` will silently treat the 400 as a successful null result instead of surfacing a parameter error.

issues.summary: POST endpoints inconsistently validate JSON bodies: WCL endpoints type-guard rigorously while user-state mutators destructure unchecked
issues.suggestion: Add small parser helpers next to parseFightRequestBody in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts (or a new src/lib/server/requestBody.ts) — e.g. `parseRecentCharacterBody`, `parseRecentReportBody`, `parseCurrentStateBody` — each returning the typed body or null, and have the three handlers return `apiError('Invalid body', 400)` on null. No new framework, just three sibling functions matching the existing convention.
evidence: wclGraphQL.ts: parseFightRequestBody narrows fightID/code/startTime/endTime via explicit typeof checks; fights/+server.ts and player-details/+server.ts repeat the same pattern manually for their narrower bodies.
recent-characters/+server.ts POST line ~38: `const { characterName, realm, region } = await request.json();` — no validation, values flow straight into addUserRecent and string templating.
recent-reports/+server.ts POST: `const { code, title, guild, owner } = await request.json();` — same pattern.
current-state/+server.ts POST: `const { urlParams }: Omit<CurrentState, 'timestamp'> = await request.json();` — type assertion without a runtime check.
Result: WCL endpoints reliably 400 on bad input; mutator endpoints either 500 on the inevitable downstream throw or persist garbage.

issues.summary: Sibling external-proxy endpoints handle failures inconsistently: blizzard wraps with try/catch, raiderio is unguarded
issues.suggestion: Wrap the raiderio handler body in try/catch matching blizzard: log via logServerError('api/raiderio', 'request failed', error) and return `apiError('Failed to fetch data')`. Keep the existing 'Failed to fetch data' message for the non-ok response branch.
evidence: raiderio/+server.ts has no try/catch: `const response = await fetch(apiUrl); ... const rawData: RaiderIoResponse = await response.json();` — a network throw or non-JSON body becomes an unlogged SvelteKit 500.
blizzard/+server.ts wraps the same shape of work in try/catch with logServerError('api/blizzard', ...) and returns apiError on failure.
Both are documented as being on the score-resolution fallback chain (see character-score/+server.ts), so a developer would reasonably expect the same failure surface from both.

issues.summary: /api/blizzard returns raw upstream error text in the error envelope while sibling proxies return generic messages
issues.suggestion: In the catch block of blizzard/+server.ts, log the upstream detail via logServerError('api/blizzard', 'request failed', error) (already done) but return `apiError('Failed to fetch character data from Blizzard.', 502)` to match the WCL proxy convention. Keep the rich error in the server log.
evidence: blizzard/+server.ts: `throw new Error(`Summary endpoint error: ${await summaryResponse.text()}`);` then catches and returns `apiError(error instanceof Error ? error.message : 'Internal Server Error.')`, so clients can receive a multi-line Blizzard JSON inside `error`.
Peer endpoints (fights, player-details, raiderio, all WCL handlers via handleWclFightRequest) return short generic strings like 'Failed to fetch fights from API.' regardless of upstream content.
Result: a frontend rendering `data.error` shows a polished message for one provider and a JSON dump for another.

issues.summary: `fightFilter='kills_no_deaths'` means different things in /api/spec-top-players vs /api/top-players
issues.suggestion: Change spec-top-players to use the same and(isKill=true, deathCount=0) clause for `kills_no_deaths` to match top-players. Better, extract a single `buildFightFilterCondition(fightFilter)` helper next to the specPerformance schema and call it from both handlers.
evidence: spec-top-players/+server.ts lines 33-44: `kills_no_deaths` and `kills_all` both reduce to `eq(specPerformance.isKill, true)` — the deathCount portion is silently ignored.
top-players/+server.ts lines 36-43: `kills_no_deaths` is `and(isKill=true, deathCount=0)`, `kills_all` is `isKill=true` — the documented distinction is honored.
Both endpoints query the same `specPerformance` table and present the same enum to clients.

issues.summary: Authenticated POST handlers skip request-body validation that public POST siblings apply, splitting the trust boundary
issues.suggestion: Add a tiny shared helper in src/lib/server/ (e.g., `parseJsonBody<T>(request, validate): Promise<T | Response>`) that wraps `request.json().catch(() => null)` and returns apiError('Invalid request body', 400) on failure, then thread it through the three authenticated POST handlers (current-state, recent-characters, recent-reports). This matches what the public sibling endpoints already do and makes the trust boundary symmetric without introducing a new validation framework.
evidence: current-state/+server.ts:50 — `const { urlParams }: Omit<CurrentState, 'timestamp'> = await request.json();` — no try/catch around parse, no shape check; a non-JSON or malformed body throws an unhandled SyntaxError that the surrounding try/catch then logs as a generic 'save failed'.

recent-characters/+server.ts:40 — `const { characterName, realm, region } = await request.json();` — values flow straight into addUserRecent without any type/length checks; characterName is also interpolated into the entity key `${characterName}-${realm}` (line 43).
recent-reports/+server.ts:45 — `const { code, title, guild, owner } = await request.json();` — same shape, no validation.

Public siblings consistently use the safer pattern: death-events/+server.ts:66 `const body = parseFightRequestBody(await request.json().catch(() => null));`, fights/+server.ts:38 `const body = (await request.json().catch(() => null)) as { code?: unknown } | null;`, player-details/+server.ts:26 same pattern.

issues.summary: Failure branch dumps five named response payloads with console.error({ ... }) instead of using the project logger.
issues.suggestion: Replace with a single scoped log that names the failing endpoint(s) and uses src/lib/utils/clientLog.ts (logClientError) — drop the bulk object dump, or guard it behind an explicit debug flag.
evidence: Line ~259: console.error({ damageData, healingData, castData, bossData, playerDetailsData }) inside the !response.ok branch of handleFightSelection.
All five payloads are full server responses; output to the user's browser console on every failed fight load.

issues.summary: SvelteKitAuth config ships with debug: true unconditionally, producing verbose auth-flow logging in production.
issues.suggestion: Gate on `process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production'` (matching the cookie `secure` flag a few lines below) so debug output is dev-only.
evidence: Line 51: `debug: true,` is set as a literal constant in the auth config (no env gate).

issues.summary: Several one-line // comments in raid logs page restate the next line of code without adding context.
issues.suggestion: Delete the comments — the function names already say what the next line does. Keep comments only where they explain a non-obvious constraint.
evidence: '// Update URL with fight parameter' immediately above updateUrlParams(codeToFetch, fight.id).

issues.summary: Five components still use `run` from `svelte/legacy` instead of native `$effect`
issues.suggestion: Replace each `run(() => …)` with `$effect(() => …)` (or `$derived` where the body is pure). damageChart's mix of `run()` and `$effect()` in one file is the strongest signal; converting that file alone will leave a single consistent reactive idiom across the component tree, after which `svelte/legacy` can be banned via an ESLint `no-restricted-imports` rule.
evidence: authButton.svelte:2 `import { run } from 'svelte/legacy';` then `run(() => { ... })` at line 35 inside a component that otherwise uses $state/$derived/$props.
charts/damageChart.svelte has 5 `run(() => …)` blocks (lines 121, 129, 322, 451) coexisting with native `$effect(() => …)` at line 545 in the same file.
calculator/keystoneTable.svelte:2 imports `run as run_1` — the codemod's suffix-rename pattern, indicating untouched automated output.
All other components in the codebase already express reactive side effects with `$effect(() => …)` (e.g. charts/deathHotspots.svelte:54).

issues.summary: Deprecated `$app/stores` still imported in 7 components/routes despite SvelteKit 2 `$app/state` being the new idiom
issues.suggestion: Land the documented follow-up: switch imports to `import { page } from '$app/state'` and replace `$page.x` with `page.x` (it's a runes-friendly reactive object, not a store). Update src/test-setup.ts to mock `$app/state` instead. This removes the only remaining store-subscription idiom in the tree and aligns with the rest of the rune-native code.
evidence: All seven files import `{ page }` (or `page as pageStore`) from `$app/stores` and then read it via the store-subscription `$page` syntax (e.g. authButton.svelte:30 `$derived($page.data.session …)`, seo.svelte:21 `$derived(base + $page.url.pathname)`).

docs/MIGRATION_PLAN.md Phase 2 step 6: 'Fix `$app/stores` deprecation: new is `$app/state` with `page`/`navigating` as reactive objects. For now keep `$app/stores` (still works in Svelte 5) — migrate in a follow-up.' The follow-up is not yet visible in the tree.
src/test-setup.ts:27 mocks `$app/stores`, anchoring the legacy import path in test infrastructure.

issues.summary: src/lib/db/ flattens two distinct databases (raid analytics + user/auth) into one 10-file directory
issues.suggestion: Split into src/lib/db/raid/ (schema.ts, connection.ts, index.ts, _helpers.ts) and src/lib/db/user/ (userSchema.ts→schema.ts, userDb.ts→db.ts, users.ts, userCharacters.ts→characters.ts, userRecents.ts→recents.ts, bnetAccount.ts). The rename also removes the redundant 'user' prefix on every file inside db/user/.
evidence: Two Drizzle configs (drizzle.config.ts + drizzle.user.config.ts) and two migration trees (drizzle/ + drizzle/user/) confirm two distinct databases.

Inside src/lib/db/, the only signal of which DB a file belongs to is a 'user' filename prefix (userSchema.ts, userDb.ts, userCharacters.ts, userRecents.ts) plus bnetAccount.ts; raid-analytics tables sit in schema.ts/index.ts/connection.ts with no matching prefix.
directory_profiles shows db/ at file_count=10 with mixed concerns — above the rubric's 'flat directory overload' guidance once the 'two databases' role is known.

issues.suggestion: Adopt the existing connection.ts factory: rewrite getRaidDb and getUserDb as 5-line wrappers that pass (label, envVarName) into a shared createDrizzlePostgres helper (or delete connection.ts and inline a single internal helper in db/index.ts that both factories call). issues.summary: src/routes/api/top-players/+server.ts mixes param parsing, role spec lists, branched query building with duplicated select shapes, and dedup in one handler
issues.suggestion: Split into a small server module (e.g. $lib/server/topPlayers.ts) that exposes one query function (encounterId, difficulty, fightFilter, role, metric) -> ranked, deduped rows, and have the route handler only do request parsing + apiOk/apiError. Lift HEALER_SPECS/TANK_SPECS into $lib/types/specs (or wherever spec metadata already lives). Replace Map<string, any> with the actual select-row type.

The role-spec data (healer/tank specs) is hardcoded structurally in this one route rather than living next to the spec metadata used elsewhere.

issues.summary: src/routes/api/character-reports/+server.ts (343 LOC) bundles three WCL queries, two data sources, fetchers, dedup, and ranking in one route file
issues.suggestion: Extract the WCL query strings, fetchers, dedup, and guild ranking into a $lib/server/characterReports.ts module that exposes getCharacterReports(userId) -> CharacterReportsResponse. Leave the route file as a thin wrapper: requireSession + apiOk/apiError. Collapse GUILD_RECENT_REPORTS_QUERY and GUILD_REPORTS_QUERY since their differences (if any) aren't visible from current usage.

By comparison, character-score/+server.ts (40 LOC) delegates to $lib/server/scoreSources — the same shape would work here.

issues.reasoning: SvelteKit framework entry point — loaded by the framework (not via import graph). 'Async functions without await' is correct for handleSecurity / handleSession (their async-ness is the Handle contract, not awaiting work). No design defect.
Not orphaned — getValidBattleNetUserToken is imported by src/lib/data/myWowRoster.ts (used twice). Detector missed the import.
SvelteKit route file (+server.ts) — entry point, no importers expected. The mixed-responsibilities aspect is captured separately in the confirmed character_reports_route_overload finding.
SvelteKit route file — orphan signal is a framework false positive. Reading the file shows a thin handler delegating to $lib/server/scoreSources; no design defect.
SvelteKit route file — orphan signal is a framework false positive. The real design issue (handler overload) is captured in the confirmed top_players_handler_overload finding.
SvelteKit page server entry point (+page.server.ts) — auto-loaded by the framework, no importers expected. File is a clean PageServerLoad + Actions definition.
SvelteKit route file — entry point, framework false positive on orphan.

Vitest test utility module — referenced by tests via path alias / direct import; orphan detector misses test-only graph.

issues.summary: fetchRuns / fetchWowSummary document `@throws Never` but JSON parsing happens outside the try/catch, so SyntaxError can propagate.
issues.suggestion: Either widen the try/catch in fetchRuns and fetchWowSummary to cover `response.json()` (returning `{ kind: 'error', status: response.status }` or `status: 0` on parse failure) or weaken the JSDoc to `@throws SyntaxError on malformed JSON; otherwise never throws`. The first option keeps callers' branching simple and matches the rest of the discriminated-union style used in the file.
evidence: characterData.ts:42 JSDoc on fetchRuns: `@throws Never — network failures are surfaced as { kind: 'error', status: 0 }`. Implementation wraps only `response = await fetch(url)` in try/catch (lines 49-55); the subsequent `await response.json()` (line 60) and the `data.runs.slice(...)` mapping run with no error handler. A non-JSON body (HTML 503 page, truncated response) throws a SyntaxError that bypasses the documented FetchRunsResult contract.

characterData.ts:92 fetchWowSummary repeats the same pattern: `@throws Never — non-OK responses are surfaced as { kind: 'error' }`, but `await response.json()` at line 110 is outside the fetch try/catch.

issues.summary: refreshBattleNetToken's docstring promises persistence but the DB write's catch block silently swallows failures, so 'fresh token returned' does not imply 'tokens persisted'.
issues.suggestion: Either (a) align the doc with the behavior — note that persistence is best-effort and a stale `expires_at` may persist — and route the failure through `logServerError('bnet/refresh', ...)` so a recurring failure is observable, or (b) honor the documented contract by letting the persistence error propagate (or returning null). Option (a) is the smaller change and matches the codebase's existing pattern for best-effort writes (auth.ts:96 logs and continues with a comment).
evidence: bnetAccount.ts:39-40 JSDoc: 'Refresh an expired Battle.net OAuth token using the stored refresh_token and persist the new values on the accounts row. Returns the fresh access token, or null when the refresh failed.' The contract pairs return value with persistence.

bnetAccount.ts:88-99 implementation: the inner `try { db.update(accounts).set({...}) } catch { /* Ignore persistence failures here; callers can still use the fresh token. */ }` returns `payload.access_token` after a swallowed write. There is no log line, no telemetry, and no signal in the return type that persistence failed — the next call to getValidBattleNetUserToken will see stale stored tokens and trigger another refresh, which is the exact case the doc says won't happen.

issues.summary: src/lib/db/index.ts re-exports a partial subset of the DB API (`userSchema`, `users`, `userRecents`) while omitting `bnetAccount` and `userCharacters`; no caller imports through it.
issues.suggestion: Pick one of: (a) delete the `export *` block entirely and keep getRaidDb only — concrete imports are already the project convention, or (b) make the barrel exhaustive by adding `export * from './userCharacters'` and `export * from './bnetAccount'`. Option (a) is simpler and matches the existing import pattern across auth.ts, myWowRoster.ts and the API routes.
evidence: index.ts:55-58 contains `export * from './userSchema'; export * from './users'; export * from './userRecents'; export { getUserDb } from './userDb';` — bnetAccount.ts (getValidBattleNetUserToken, getBattleNetAccount) and userCharacters.ts (getStoredCharacters, replaceStoredCharacters, etc.) are equally public but missing.

Grep across src/ shows every consumer imports concretely: auth.ts → '$lib/db/users.js' + '$lib/db/userSchema.js'; myWowRoster.ts → '$lib/db/bnetAccount' + '$lib/db/userCharacters'; routes/api/* → '$lib/db/userRecents.js'. Zero call sites use '$lib/db' or '$lib/db/index'. The barrel is effectively dead but its partiality misleads anyone who reads it as the public API contract for src/lib/db.

issues.summary: fetchReportsForGuild loops over two byte-identical GraphQL queries, and its catch returns [] inside the loop, so the fallback path is dead
issues.suggestion: Delete GUILD_REPORTS_QUERY (it is a duplicate of GUILD_RECENT_REPORTS_QUERY) and inline the single query into fetchReportsForGuild without a loop. If a real fallback was intended (e.g., a different filter window), define a second query that actually differs and change the catch from `return []` to `continue` so the loop can fall through to the next attempt.
evidence: GUILD_RECENT_REPORTS_QUERY and GUILD_REPORTS_QUERY have the same operation, same selection set, same `limit: 8`, same arguments — they differ only in the `query` operation name.

`for (const query of queries)` iterates [identical1, identical2]; on success it returns; on catch it `return []` (not `continue`), so the second iteration can never execute and would return the same data anyway.

issues.summary: Empty if-branch in top-players whose only content is a placeholder comment is a dead conditional
issues.suggestion: Delete the entire `if (results.length > 0) { ... }` block. If debugging probes are wanted later, add them under a real flag (e.g. `if (debug) logServerInfo(...)`); a commented-out probe with no body is just noise that obscures the linear flow of the handler.
evidence: `if (results.length > 0) { // Sample record available for debugging if needed }` — the conditional has no statements, only a comment that paraphrases the condition itself; the branch has no observable effect at runtime.

issues.summary: seoTitle is declared and returned from load() but never assigned, so it is permanently undefined
issues.suggestion: Either assign a meaningful seoTitle inside the score-branch (e.g. `seoTitle = `${score} Mythic+ Rating Calculator`;`) and surface it in the page <svelte:head>, or delete the `seoTitle` declaration and the `seoTitle` field from the return object. Carrying an always-undefined field through the data pipeline implies a contract the code does not actually fulfill.
evidence: `let seoTitle: string | undefined;` is declared at the top of load(); the `if (score && Number.isFinite(score) && score > 0)` block assigns seoDescription and seoKeywords but never seoTitle; the function returns { form, seoTitle, seoDescription, seoKeywords }, so seoTitle is always undefined.

issues.summary: Five SvelteKit handlers/loads are marked async but contain no await, wrapping purely synchronous work in an unnecessary microtask
issues.suggestion: Drop the `async` keyword on these handlers/loads. SvelteKit's RequestHandler and PageServerLoad both accept `MaybePromise<...>`, so a sync function is type-correct and removes the cosmetic Promise wrapper, making it obvious at the signature level that the handler does no I/O.
evidence: src/routes/sitemap.xml/+server.ts: `export const GET = async () => { ... new Response(sitemap, ...) }` — only string assembly, no await.
src/routes/api/calculate-runs/+server.ts: `export const GET: RequestHandler = async ({ url }) => { ... }` — only parseInt, calculateKeystoneBreakdown, a for-sum, and apiOk; no await.
src/routes/beta-raidtesting/+page.server.ts: `export const load: PageServerLoad = async () => { throw redirect(308, '/raid'); };` — single sync throw.
src/routes/raid/boss/+page.server.ts: same pattern (`async () => { throw redirect(307, ...) }`).
src/routes/raid/boss/[bossname]/+page.server.ts: `async ({ params }) => { const boss = bosses.find(...); if (!boss) throw redirect(...); return { bossId, boss } }` — no await.

issues.summary: getUserRecents<T> casts unvalidated JSONB to RecentItem<T>[], so the generic promises a shape the runtime never checks
issues.suggestion: Either drop the <T> generic and return `RecentItem<unknown>[]` so callers must narrow with the existing RecentEntityData discriminated union, or have getUserRecents accept the discriminator (`type: RecentEntityType`) and at runtime tag each row with the matching union variant before returning. Concretely: change the signature to `getUserRecents(userId: string, type?: RecentEntityType, limit?: number): Promise<RecentItem<unknown>[]>` and let callers `switch` on `r.type` against RecentEntityData.
evidence: Lines 138-163: getUserRecents<T = unknown>(...): Promise<RecentItem<T>[]> performs `getUserDb().select().from(userRecents)...` and returns `recents as unknown as RecentItem<T>[]`.

The `entityData` column is JSONB and is written by addUserRecent with `entityData: unknown`; nothing in the read path narrows it to T, so callers passing e.g. `getUserRecents<CharacterRecentData>` receive items typed as CharacterRecentData but holding arbitrary parsed JSON.

issues.summary: Map<string, any> discards the Drizzle row type that the surrounding select() already provides
issues.suggestion: Define `type SpecRow = Awaited<typeof query>[number]` (or extract the select shape into a named type) and replace `Map<string, any>` with `Map<string, SpecRow>`. The `result[metricKey]` access works because both keys exist on the row type as `number | null`.
evidence: Line 112: `const playerMap = new Map<string, any>();`
Lines 64-99: both `query` branches use `database.select({ id: ..., player_name: ..., dps: ..., hps: ..., ... })`, producing a fully-inferred row type accessible via `Awaited<typeof query>[number]`.
Lines 122-125 then do `result[metricKey] ?? 0` where `metricKey: 'dps' | 'hps'` — the `any` exists only to make this index access compile.

issues.summary: src/app.d.ts contains an implementation function with an implicit-any `context` parameter inside a declaration file
issues.suggestion: Delete the `export async function post(context) {...}` block from src/app.d.ts (it is dead and shouldn't live in a declaration file at all), and tighten `waitUntil: (promise: Promise<any>) => void` to `waitUntil: (promise: Promise<unknown>) => void` since callers never read its resolved value.
evidence: Lines 21: `waitUntil: (promise: Promise<any>) => void;` uses `any` for the platform context contract.

Lines 28-30: `export async function post(context) { const counter = context.platform.env.COUNTER.idFromName('A'); }` is a real function body in a `.d.ts` file with no parameter type — leftover from the Cloudflare Workers template that has no SvelteKit role.

issues.summary: damageChart.svelte threads Chart.js plugin internals through repeated `as any` casts instead of typing the annotation plugin
issues.suggestion: Import the chartjs-plugin-annotation types (`AnnotationOptions` from `chartjs-plugin-annotation`) and type `annotations: AnnotationOptions[]`. Inside the legend onClick, type the receiver as `LegendElement<'line'>` (the `this` context Chart.js documents) so the four `(this as any)` casts disappear; the `options.plugins as any` block can then be replaced by initialising `plugins.annotation` once in the literal.
evidence: Line 178: `const options: ChartOptions<'line'> & { plugins: { annotation: { annotations: any[] } } } = $state({...})`.

Lines 243-265: `(this as any).chart.getDatasetMeta(...)`, `(this as any).chart.data.datasets[...].hidden`, `(this as any).chart.update()` inside legend onClick — these are typed by Chart.js's `LegendItem` callbacks.
Lines 547-548: `(options.plugins as any).annotation = (options.plugins as any).annotation || { annotations: [] };` and `(options.plugins as any).annotation.annotations = annotations as any;`.

add.header: Near-leaf coupling matrix with no cycles
add.description: structure.coupling_matrix shows only two cross-directory edges in the entire src tree (auth->types, db->server via a single dead file). Layer direction is uniform routes -> lib, and lib/db, lib/server, lib/auth are effectively leaves — a developer can change a leaf without ripple risk.

add.header: Routes consume lib via stable barrel and direct imports
add.description: Raid handlers consistently use `$lib/db` for getRaidDb and `$lib/server/apiResponses`/`$lib/server/logger` for response/error plumbing, giving cross-module handoffs a predictable shape per route family.

add.header: Documented two-DB boundary partially eroded at db/index.ts
add.description: schema.ts and connection.ts headers describe a strict two-database split, but db/index.ts barrel re-exports user-DB modules and consumers reach past it for user-DB needs, so the boundary lives in docs but not at the import surface.

add.header: createDrizzlePostgres factory has no callers
add.description: db/connection.ts documents itself as the owner of connection settings, yet getRaidDb (db/index.ts) and getUserDb (db/userDb.ts) each inline the same postgres() option block. The factory is a documented contract that runtime ignores, and it is also the sole reason db -> server appears in the coupling matrix.

add.header: Two store roots: src/stores.ts vs $lib/stores/
add.description: Calculator state (apiPopup, dungeonData, wowSummaryStore) sits at the top-level src/ root and is imported via ../../stores, while recentCharacters/recentReports live under $lib/stores/. There is no documented rule for the split, and only calculator components cross the boundary.

add.header: seo.svelte and footer.svelte are facades, not hubs
add.description: Both components hit 8 importers in the structure summary, but they are thin shared layout/SEO surfaces with stable contracts. Treating their fan-in as architectural hub-ness would mistake intentional reuse for coupling debt.

add.header: Dual-database split is principled and documented inline
add.description: raid analytics (DATABASE_URL, schema.ts, drizzle.config.ts, drizzle/) and user/auth (DATABASE_USER_URL, userSchema.ts, drizzle.user.config.ts, drizzle/user/) are deliberately separate Postgres instances and userSchema.ts's header explains why. This is a strong domain boundary even where index.ts later blurs it on export.

add.header: Top-level project contract missing
add.description: README.md is empty. PRODUCT.md and DESIGN.md exist but nothing names them as the entry doc, so the first thing a new engineer opens tells them nothing about what the repo is, what boots first, or where conventions live.

add.header: Documented ownership claims drift from runtime
add.description: db/connection.ts advertises itself as the owner of shared postgres client settings but is unused — getRaidDb and getUserDb still inline the same options. db/index.ts and userSchema.ts disagree about whether the two-database boundary is observable at the import surface. Decomposition reads worse than it is because docs over-promise.

add.header: Two component roots without a documented rule
add.description: src/components/ holds feature components and src/lib/components/ui/ holds shadcn primitives. The split is defensible but never written down; src/lib/index.ts is a placeholder. New components have no canonical home.

add.header: routes/api uses three naming idioms
add.description: Event-type (boss-events, cast-events), provider passthroughs (blizzard, raiderio), and aggregation/verb endpoints (top-players, browse-logs, calculate-runs) coexist. The mix isn't accidental — different concerns warrant different shapes — but no documented namespace prefix makes the split predictable.

add.header: Uniform camelCase across lib/ subdirectories
add.description: All 244 sampled function names across src/, auth/, calculations/, data/, db/, server/, stores/, ui/ are camelCase with a small consistent verb vocabulary (get/create/fetch/load/is). There is no casing island in the production code.

add.header: Named-export-only module pattern
add.description: Every lib/ subdirectory uses named exports; class-based modules are restricted to a few intentional places (server/, types/, ui/). New contributors can rely on `import { X } from '$lib/...'` without checking export style per file.

add.header: Token cache factory pattern is deliberate
add.description: lib/auth/tokenCacheBase.ts is the single source for the OAuth client-credentials cache; tokenCache.ts and blizzardTokenCache.ts are intentionally tiny config files with no `type` declaration. The mechanical sibling-behavior 'missing type' signal here is a false positive of the factory layout, not drift.

add.header: Shared WCL handler centralises route lifecycle
add.description: handleWclFightRequest in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts captures the 400/502/500 ladder for WCL-backed endpoints; four of five WCL endpoints use it, making the convention clear by repetition.

add.header: death-events bypasses the shared WCL handler
add.description: src/routes/api/death-events/+server.ts hand-rolls parseFightRequestBody + executeWclQuery + a manual WclQueryError try/catch instead of using handleWclFightRequest like its siblings. Single-file outlier in an otherwise consistent cluster.

add.header: snake_case vs camelCase split in /api response shapes
add.description: DB-backed /api routes (spec-statistics, spec-top-players, top-players, browse-logs, damage-average, death-hotspots) project snake_case fields; WCL-backed routes return camelCase. The split is undocumented and downstream code re-spreads the snake_case keys, propagating drift.

add.header: Documented server log shape via logServerError
add.description: src/lib/server/logger.ts establishes a single '[scope] message' format with a JSDoc that tells handlers exactly how to use logServerError/logServerWarn/handleApiError. When followed, every server failure is greppable by scope tag and carries the original error object — a small, deliberate seam that pays off across the codebase.

add.header: WclQueryError is a typed upstream-failure boundary
add.description: src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts wraps every WCL transport and GraphQL failure in a WclQueryError with kind: 'http' | 'graphql', which lets handleWclFightRequest map the whole failure class to a single HTTP 502 — a clean, intentional layer translation when callers actually use the helper.

add.header: Result-type Network boundary in src/lib/data
add.description: fetchRuns/fetchWowSummary in src/lib/data/characterData.ts use discriminated `{ kind: 'ok' | 'error' | 'empty' }` results with `@throws Never` JSDoc, deliberately swallowing network failures into a typed status. This is a documented layer choice (Result in client-data, throw in API), not drift, and keeps UI code free of try/catch noise.

add.header: requireSession discriminated-union response
add.description: src/lib/server/requireSession returns `{ response } | { session }` so every protected handler discriminates on `'response' in auth` and short-circuits with an early return. Auth failures propagate identically across all handlers without exceptions, which is a small but very consistent pattern across the routes.

add.header: handleApiError adoption stalled at ~50%
add.description: logger.ts documents handleApiError as the canonical catch-block helper, but only 6 of ~14 API route handlers use it; the rest reproduce its body as `logServerError(...) + apiError(...)`. Same shape on the wire, parallel idiom in source — a classic helper-drift pattern that propagates because the manual version 'works'.

add.header: Server-side modules outside src/lib/server bypass logServerError
add.description: myWowRoster.ts and db/bnetAccount.ts run on the server but emit free-form console.error strings (and one empty catch), breaking the '[scope] message' grep contract that logger.ts documents. The helpers exist; they are not adopted because no lint rule or review checklist enforces them.

add.header: character-reports collapses errors into empty success
add.description: The outer catch in src/routes/api/character-reports/+server.ts returns the same `{reports:[], guilds:[]}` payload that a legitimate empty roster returns, so a client cannot distinguish 'no characters' from 'WCL or DB exploded'. This contradicts the apiError-on-failure contract every other handler follows.

add.header: WCL status code split (500 vs 502)
add.description: handleWclFightRequest maps WclQueryError to 502, but fights/death-events/character-reports inline their own try/catch and return apiError() default 500 for the same error class. Same upstream failure, two different HTTP statuses depending on which file the route lives in.

add.header: Verb-prefix vocabulary is consistent across lib
add.description: fetch* names HTTP/network retrieval, get* names synchronous/db lookups, is*/has* names predicates that actually return boolean, and handle* is reserved for UI event handlers. A developer can predict the I/O shape of a function from its prefix alone.

add.header: Domain vocabulary is rich and used directly
add.description: Module and function names use the actual problem-space terms (wcl, raiderio, blizzard, keystone, fight, report, ability, oauth, tokenCache) rather than generic placeholders, so call sites read as domain operations rather than data plumbing.

add.header: `response` is the established fetch-result convention
add.description: Across at least a dozen call sites in components, lib/data, lib/server, lib/auth and routes, the result of fetch() is uniformly bound to `response`. Treat any local `res`/`resp` as the outlier, not as an acceptable shorthand.

add.header: Event-handler `handle*` is convention, not generic-verb leak
add.description: Svelte components consistently prefix UI event handlers with handle* (handleSelect, handlePageChange, handleSignIn, handleBossChange, etc.). Used 50+ times across components and routes — this is a project convention and should not be flagged as a generic verb.

add.header: Naming defects are local closures, not module-level
add.description: The three open naming issues all live inside single functions (doSave inside saveToAPI, hide inside hideWowheadTooltips, res inside deathHotspots fetch). Module-level exports follow conventions cleanly — the friction is concentrated in inner-scope shorthand.

add.header: WCL fight handler factory pattern
add.description: handleWclFightRequest in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts standardises body parsing, query execution, and 400/502/500 error mapping for per-fight WCL endpoints. Adoption by 4 of 5 sibling routes makes the abstraction earned rather than ceremonial.

add.header: Token cache factory has two real implementations
add.description: createTokenCache in tokenCacheBase.ts is consumed by both WCL (tokenCache.ts) and Blizzard (blizzardTokenCache.ts) with distinct env reads and endpoints, so the indirection delivers genuine reuse and concurrency safety rather than single-implementation ceremony.

add.header: Auth modules carry dead isTokenExpired re-exports
add.description: oauth.ts and blizzardOauth.ts each re-export isTokenExpired from oauthBase but no caller imports those symbols — tokenCacheBase reaches for the original. The re-exports add naming surface without substitution value and should be pruned.

add.header: Single-vendor stacks per concern
add.description: Auth (Auth.js trio), DB (Drizzle + postgres-js), charts (chart.js + svelte-chartjs + plugins), and forms (zod + sveltekit-superforms + formsnap) each pick one ecosystem with no competing library, keeping the dependency surface small and predictable.

add.header: Lazy-loaded chartjs-plugin-zoom
add.description: averageChart.svelte and damageChart.svelte import chartjs-plugin-zoom via `await import(...)` rather than statically, deferring the plugin's weight until the chart actually needs interactivity.

add.header: clsx + tailwind-merge are complementary, not duplicate
add.description: Both are used together in src/lib/utils.ts to build the `cn()` helper — clsx joins class names, tailwind-merge resolves Tailwind conflicts. This is the canonical shadcn pattern and not a sign of redundancy.

add.header: All runtime deps actively imported
add.description: Each of the 12 entries in `dependencies` resolves to at least one src/ import, with no stale runtime packages observed. Drift, when it exists, is contained to devDependencies (e.g. @types/better-sqlite3) which the rubric excludes from scoring.

add.header: dbOperation wraps every db function uniformly
add.description: src/lib/db/_helpers.ts exposes a 6-line try/catch-log-rethrow helper that every exported function in src/lib/db/* (users.ts, userRecents.ts, bnetAccount.ts, userCharacters.ts) wraps its body in. Internal control flow across the db layer is consequently identical: linear awaits inside dbOperation, no inline try/catch sprawl.

add.header: Per-fight endpoints reduced to declarative transforms
add.description: handleWclFightRequest in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts owns body validation, GraphQL execution and error mapping, so src/routes/api/{boss,cast,damage,death,healing}-events/+server.ts each contain only a query string, a transform callback and a typed result interface — no inline branching, no duplicated try/catch.

add.header: Discriminated unions instead of nullable returns
add.description: Data fetchers (characterData.ts fetchRuns/fetchWowSummary) and store backends (AddRecentReportOutcome, AddRecentCharacterOutcome) return tagged unions like {kind:'ok'|'empty'|'error'} or {status:'ok'|'unauthorized'|'unavailable'}. Callers branch on the tag instead of re-deriving state from null + status code, which keeps each branch's body short and explicit.

add.header: Resource cleanup via try/finally on timed fetches
add.description: fetchWithTimeout (scoreSources.ts) and fetchRegion (myWowRoster.ts) both pair AbortController + setTimeout with try/finally clearTimeout, so the timer is reliably cleared even on success or thrown errors. Edge-case handling is precise without spreading null checks across the call.

add.header: Endpoint local sprawl concentrates in two routes
add.description: top-players/+server.ts and character-reports/+server.ts predate the shared-helper migration and carry duplicated select blocks, dead diagnostic state, untyped dedup maps and a phantom fallback loop. The rest of src/routes/api/* is uniformly thin around handleWclFightRequest or apiOk/apiError, so these two are real outliers, not the house style.

add.header: async_no_await pattern is mostly framework signatures
add.description: scan_evidence flagged 29 files with async functions that never await, but the bulk are SvelteKit RequestHandler / load / Handle signatures whose async-ness is required by the framework type contract even when the body is synchronous. Treating these as low-level defects would be a false positive.

add.header: Shared response/auth/log seams are coherent
add.description: apiError/apiOk, requireSession's {session}|{response} union, and logServerError/handleApiError together form a small, type-safe seam library that most route handlers compose cleanly. New routes have an obvious shape to copy from neighbours.

add.header: WCL fight handlers compose through one orchestrator
add.description: handleWclFightRequest in src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts owns body validation (parseFightRequestBody), token + transport (executeWclQuery), upstream-error typing (WclQueryError), and 400/502/500 mapping. Most fight routes (boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, healing-events) read as a single composition expression.

add.header: Provider plumbing factored into createTokenCache and createDrizzlePostgres
add.description: Both WCL and Battle.net token caches share createTokenCache (src/lib/auth/tokenCacheBase.ts) including in-flight-promise dedup for refresh. The DB-client config has the parallel factory createDrizzlePostgres (src/lib/db/connection.ts) — though its two intended callers don't yet use it.

add.header: DB row→DTO translation is duplicated across handlers
add.description: Eight DB-backed +server.ts files each own an inline `.select({ snake_case: column })` plus `.map(row => ({...}))` translator. The schema-to-API boundary has no single home, so a column or contract change requires touching every consumer.

add.header: Catch-block translation has two equivalent forms
add.description: Routes split roughly evenly between `handleApiError(scope, error, msg)` and `logServerError(scope, ..., error); return apiError(msg)`. Both produce identical output, but the inconsistency means readers must recognize two shapes for the same intent.

add.header: Test risk inversion: trivia covered, core untested
add.description: The suite covers utility helpers (cn, extractWarcraftLogsReportCode, ability metadata, reveal action) well, but skips every high-risk path (rating math, 22 API handlers, OAuth/token cache, hooks.server.ts, server loads). Tests track easy-to-write modules, not high-importer or business-critical ones.

add.header: Several tests are tautologies that lift coverage without signal
add.description: drizzleConfigs.test.ts restates config literals, formIndex.test.ts asserts barrel re-exports, dbIndex.test.ts asserts `typeof === 'function'`. These tests pass whenever the file exists and parses, so they catch nothing a typecheck wouldn't already catch.

add.header: Component tests couple to copy and Tailwind classes
add.description: page.test.ts and contentShowcase.test.ts assert exact marketing strings, exact image dimensions, exact referral URL, and exact Tailwind class fragments. A copy or styling change forces a test edit even though no behavior changed — high churn, low signal.

add.header: performance.test.ts is a fabricated workload with timing thresholds
add.description: It defines its own heavyComputation/processLargeDataset/simulateApiCall and gates CI on absolute wall-clock and heap-growth thresholds. It tests no production code and is timing-fragile on shared runners; the heap-growth assertion depends on `global.gc` which vitest does not expose by default.

add.header: No HTTP-handler / load-function test harness
add.description: There is no shared RequestEvent fixture, no msw or fetch mock, and no test helper for invoking a +server.ts handler. The absence of harness makes each per-handler test expensive to write, which is why the 22-handler coverage gap stays open.

add.header: Snapshot overuse is not a failure mode here
add.description: Despite the rubric flagging snapshot overuse, this codebase uses zero snapshot tests. The fragility comes from explicit string/class assertions, not from `toMatchSnapshot`.

add.header: Real unit tests exist for pure helpers
add.description: abilityMetadata.test.ts, warcraftlogs.test.ts, interactionUtils.test.ts, and utils.test.ts test actual behavior with focused inputs/outputs and (for interactionUtils) properly mocked browser APIs. These are the template the rest of the suite should follow.

add.header: Uniform success/error envelope via apiOk/apiError
add.description: Every src/routes/api endpoint returns through apiOk/apiError from src/lib/server/apiResponses.ts, giving a single error shape `{error: string}` and a uniform 200/4xx/5xx contract — the strongest cross-cutting consistency on the surface.

add.header: Shared per-fight WCL pipeline
add.description: boss-events, cast-events, damage-events, healing-events all delegate to handleWclFightRequest in wclGraphQL.ts, which centralizes body validation (parseFightRequestBody), 502-on-WclQueryError mapping, and success wrapping. The WCL family is genuinely predictable from one example.

add.header: Centralized auth via requireSession
add.description: All authenticated endpoints (recent-characters, recent-reports, current-state, my-wow-characters, character-reports) use the same requireSession helper, producing identical 401 responses. No ad-hoc session checks scattered across handlers.

add.header: Response casing split: snake_case for DB endpoints, camelCase elsewhere
add.description: DB-fed endpoints (damage-average, death-hotspots, spec-statistics, spec-top-players, top-players, browse-logs) emit snake_case fields; non-DB endpoints (calculate-runs, character-score, boss-events, recent-characters, character-reports) emit camelCase. The split is undocumented and forces consumers to remember which corner of the API they're in.

add.header: GET vs POST chosen by upstream not by semantics
add.description: Read-only DB endpoints use GET; read-only WCL proxy endpoints (and the DB-only browse-logs) use POST with a JSON body. The verb tracks 'is there a body to send?' rather than HTTP semantics, and there's no doc explaining the rule.

add.header: Body validation rigor varies between WCL and user-state endpoints
add.description: WCL endpoints use parseFightRequestBody-style type guards. recent-characters/recent-reports/current-state POST handlers destructure JSON without runtime checks, trusting the client. Same `request.json()` API, different trust level.

add.header: Single requireSession chokepoint for protected APIs
add.description: Every user-scoped API handler (10 handlers across 5 files) routes through src/lib/server/requireSession.ts, which returns either {session} or {response: 401 apiError}. Centralising the check into one helper means a developer reviewing security only has to verify one primitive and grep for one import to enumerate the protected surface.

add.header: Identity always sourced from session, never the body
add.description: User-scoped writes (recent-characters POST, recent-reports POST, current-state POST) pass auth.session.user.id into the data layer. The request body never carries user identity, so the IDOR class of bug is structurally avoided rather than relying on per-route discipline.

add.header: Single-bit auth model — no roles or RLS
add.description: There are no permission strings, role checks, RLS policies, or service-role bypasses anywhere in the codebase; the only authorization decision is signed-in-vs-anonymous via Battle.net OAuth. This is a deliberate scope choice that eliminates whole categories of inconsistency (magic permission literals, admin bypass without audit logging) the rubric flags.

add.header: Public analytics endpoints are principled, not gaps
add.description: Unauthenticated routes under src/routes/api/ (top-players, browse-logs, boss-events, fights, damage/healing/death events, character-score, blizzard/raiderio proxies, calculate-runs) read public WoW raid analytics from the shared raid DB or proxy public upstreams keyed by query params. None of them read or write user-scoped state, so their lack of auth is by design rather than a sibling-inconsistency defect.

add.header: Page vs API auth divergence is layer-appropriate
add.description: src/routes/profile/+page.server.ts uses event.locals.getSession + throw redirect(302, '/auth/signin'), while API routes use requireSession returning a 401 JSON. This is the right per-layer split (UX redirect for HTML, machine-readable error for fetch) rather than convention drift.

add.header: Input-validation asymmetry at the trust boundary
add.description: Authenticated POST handlers (current-state, recent-characters, recent-reports) destructure request.json() with no parse-error handling or shape check, while public POST siblings (death-events, fights, player-details) consistently use `await request.json().catch(() => null)` plus a typed parser. Auth gating is uniform; the validation half of the trust boundary is not.

add.header: Shared WCL fight handler kills route boilerplate
add.description: src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts exposes handleWclFightRequest, which every per-fight endpoint (boss/cast/damage/healing/death-events) consumes; route files only declare a GraphQL string and a transform, eliminating the copy-pasted try/catch/error-shape pattern LLMs typically emit.

add.header: Centralised server logger with stable [scope] shape
add.description: src/lib/server/logger.ts forces every server log through logServerError/logServerWarn/handleApiError with a stable [scope] prefix. The module docstring records the prior drift it was created to fix, so future contributors don’t reintroduce ad-hoc string formats.

add.header: JSDoc explains WHY, not WHAT
add.description: Comments on non-trivial entry points (auth.ts signIn callback, userSchema.ts header, character-score endpoint) explain external constraints — Drizzle adapter behaviour, dual-database deployment, fallback chain semantics — rather than restating signatures. Trivial helpers carry no docstrings.

add.header: Residual LLM-style noise concentrated in raid logs page
add.description: src/routes/raid/logs=[reportcode]/+page.svelte holds most of the dimension’s remaining debt: a full-object console.error dump on the failure branch and a handful of restating // comments above self-describing function calls. The rest of the codebase is largely free of these patterns.

add.header: Major-version migration plan executed end-to-end
add.description: docs/MIGRATION_PLAN.md lays out a phased Svelte 5 / Vite 7 / Tailwind 4 / bits-ui 2 / Zod 4 migration with exit gates, and package.json shows every target version is now installed. Most migration debt visible today is small residue from a finished migration, not in-flight drift.

add.header: Svelte 5 runes are the dominant component idiom
add.description: Across components/, routes/, and lib/ the new $state / $derived / $props / $effect APIs are pervasive, with no `export let`, `$:` reactive labels, or `on:event` directives remaining. New components naturally inherit the modern style.

add.header: No TODO/FIXME migration debt or @deprecated callers
add.description: Repo-wide grep finds zero TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK or @deprecated markers tied to migration work, and no mixed .js/.ts modules. The team finishes migrations rather than leaving breadcrumbs.

add.header: svelte/legacy run() codemod residue in 5 components
add.description: authButton, logBrowser, damageChart, averageChart, and keystoneTable still call `run` from `svelte/legacy` (one alias as `run_1`), the automatic output of `npx sv migrate svelte-5`. damageChart even mixes `run()` with native `$effect()` in the same file, creating the only visible 'which idiom?' friction.

add.header: $app/stores deferred from $app/state migration
add.description: Eight files (seven components/routes plus the test mock) still import `{ page }` from `$app/stores` and use the `$page` store-subscription form, while the rest of the code is fully runes-native. MIGRATION_PLAN.md explicitly defers this swap to a follow-up.

add.header: SvelteKit route layout followed cleanly
add.description: src/routes/ adheres to SvelteKit conventions: each src/routes/api/<endpoint>/+server.ts maps directly to a URL, +layout.svelte/+page.svelte placement is consistent, and hooks.server.ts sits at the framework-mandated src/ root. A developer who knows SvelteKit can navigate the route tree without inspecting code.

add.header: Cohesive lib subpackages by concern
add.description: src/lib/auth/, src/lib/server/, src/lib/data/, src/lib/types/, and src/lib/components/ui/ each contain files that share a single role (OAuth, server-only utilities, data fetchers, type defs, shadcn primitives). Membership is predictable and the coupling_matrix shows clean inter-package edges (auth/→types/, db/→server/) with no cycles.

add.header: lib/utils.ts file vs lib/utils/ folder collision
add.description: src/lib/utils.ts (shadcn cn() + type aliases) and src/lib/utils/clientLog.ts coexist as siblings sharing the 'utils' name with unrelated contents, forcing developers to disambiguate which $lib/utils path they want.

add.header: Stores split between src/ root and lib/stores/
add.description: src/stores.ts (global UI stores) lives at the package root while src/lib/stores/ holds the recent-* stores. The directory layout offers no rule for where a new Svelte store should go, and import paths differ accordingly.

add.header: db/ flattens two distinct databases
add.description: src/lib/db/ holds 10 files spanning two separate Drizzle databases (raid analytics + user/auth). The only signal which database a file targets is a 'user' filename prefix; the directory itself does not encode the boundary that drizzle.config.ts and drizzle.user.config.ts already make explicit.

add.header: Single-file and empty folders under lib/
add.description: src/lib/actions/, src/lib/calculations/, and src/lib/utils/ each hold a single file, and src/lib/hooks/ is empty. These thin wrappers fragment the library root without carrying their weight as groupings.

add.header: DB clients constructed per-request behind factories
add.description: getRaidDb() and getUserDb() build a fresh postgres-js client on each call with `max: 1`, short idle/lifetime timeouts, and a `building` guard. This deliberately avoids module-level connections to dodge a Cloudflare Workers connection-state bug documented in connection.ts.

add.header: Env reads deferred inside closures, not at module scope
add.description: Token caches and OAuth helpers expose `readCredentials: () => env.X` callbacks rather than reading env at import. This keeps modules importable in test harnesses without setting every secret and avoids fragile boot-order from $env/dynamic/private.

add.header: Per-isolate singleton caches are documented and bounded
add.description: createTokenCache() and rosterCache (myWowRoster.ts) are intentional process-local singletons with explicit comments explaining the per-isolate fan-out, in-flight Promise sharing, and TTL. Creation order across modules does not matter because each closure owns its own state.

add.header: Sole process.env read is in auth cookie config
add.description: src/auth.ts uses `process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'` for cookie.secure while the rest of the code uses $env/dynamic/private. Minor consistency drift but it is read inside the SvelteKitAuth factory callback, so it remains deferred.

add.header: WCL fight endpoints share a single helper
add.description: boss-events/cast-events/damage-events/healing-events/death-events all delegate to handleWclFightRequest in $lib/server/wclGraphQL with a per-route { query, operation, transform } config — earned abstraction that keeps each route file ~30-80 LOC and visually identical in shape.

add.header: DB call wrapper used uniformly
add.description: Every DB-touching function in $lib/db/* (users, bnetAccount, userRecents) wraps work in dbOperation('<name>', async () => ...) from _helpers, giving consistent logging and error shape across the data layer.

add.header: Page.server.ts files stay thin
add.description: Page server loaders (e.g. rating-calculator/+page.server.ts) limit themselves to superValidate, URL parsing, and a small derived SEO payload — orchestration of heavy work lives in $lib/server modules.

add.header: Two DB factories duplicate postgres config
add.description: getRaidDb in db/index.ts and getUserDb in db/userDb.ts hold a verbatim ~30-line postgres() options block while db/connection.ts (the intended shared factory) is dead. Future client tuning has to happen in two files in lockstep, which is the kind of drift that produces subtle prod incidents.

add.header: A few route handlers carry too much
add.description: top-players/+server.ts and character-reports/+server.ts pack parsing, query string constants, branch-duplicated select shapes, dedup, and ranking into the route module — out of step with the thin-route + $lib/server-module pattern used by character-score/scoreSources and the WCL event endpoints.

add.header: Orphaned-file signal is noisy on SvelteKit/vitest
add.description: The orphaned detector flags every +server.ts, +page.server.ts, hooks.server.ts, auth.ts, and test-setup/utils file because they have no importers, but they are framework/test-runner entry points. Treat orphan signals on these paths as navigation noise, not design defects.

add.header: Uniform dbOperation invariant across db/*
add.description: src/lib/db/_helpers.ts documents an explicit success/failure contract (rethrow on error, log once at the DB layer, only return empty values on successful empty queries). Every exported DB function in users.ts, userRecents.ts, userCharacters.ts, and bnetAccount.ts wraps its work in `dbOperation('label', ...)`, so callers can rely on consistent error propagation across the entire data layer.

add.header: Discriminated unions at every async boundary
add.description: Public async helpers consistently return discriminated unions instead of nullable values or thrown errors: FetchRunsResult, FetchWowSummaryResult, AddOutcome, AddRecentReportOutcome, ScoreLookup, requireSession's `{session}|{response}`, RecentEntityData. Callers branch on a `kind`/`status`/`type` field rather than on truthiness, so the contract for each call site is visible at the type level.

add.header: Explicit @throws Never documentation pattern
add.description: characterData.ts, myWowRoster.ts, and routes/api/character-score/+server.ts use `@throws Never` JSDoc to communicate that the function intentionally collapses every failure into a return value. The pattern is rare enough that its presence is a signal of contract awareness — the dimension's residual defects are mostly drift between this strict claim and the actual exception surface.

add.header: @throws Never claims miss JSON-parse path
add.description: characterData.ts fetchRuns/fetchWowSummary both wrap only the `fetch()` call in try/catch; the subsequent `response.json()` is outside, so a malformed JSON body throws despite the JSDoc saying it never does. Small, mechanical drift between doc and implementation.

add.header: bnetAccount refresh persists best-effort, doc says it persists
add.description: bnetAccount.refreshBattleNetToken silently swallows DB persistence failures while the JSDoc promises persistence as part of the contract. The function returns a fresh token even when the next call will see stale stored values and refresh again.

add.header: db/index.ts barrel is partial and unused
add.description: index.ts re-exports userSchema/users/userRecents but omits bnetAccount and userCharacters. No caller imports through the barrel; every consumer uses concrete file paths. The barrel's partiality misleads anyone who reads it as the public API contract for src/lib/db.

add.header: Linear request handlers with awaited I/O
add.description: Most +server.ts handlers follow a uniform pattern: parse params, optional auth via requireSession (early return on failure), single awaited DB or WCL call, transform, return apiOk; errors funnel through handleApiError or logServerError + apiError. A reader can predict the shape after seeing one or two handlers.

add.header: Guard-clause-first auth and validation
add.description: Route handlers consistently use early `return apiError(...)` / `if ('response' in auth) return auth.response` guard clauses before doing real work, keeping the happy path unindented and easy to follow.

add.header: Cosmetic async wrappers in some routes
add.description: A small cluster of SvelteKit handlers and loads (sitemap, calculate-runs, beta-raidtesting, raid/boss redirects) are marked async despite doing only synchronous work. Harmless at runtime but mildly misleading at the signature level.

add.header: Detector-reported async_no_await is largely false-positive
add.description: The systemic_patterns count of 29 async_no_await files overstates the real issue: the smell detector also flags route handlers that do await internally (e.g. api/blizzard, api/death-events, api/character-score). The genuine cluster is closer to 5 files; future investigations should sample before treating the count as load-bearing.

add.header: Pockets of abandoned-in-progress logic
add.description: Three small but clear clarity defects sit in otherwise clean modules: character-reports has a fallback-query loop over two identical queries with a return-from-catch that prevents fallback anyway; top-players has an empty if-branch whose only content is a placeholder comment; rating-calculator's load returns a seoTitle field that is declared but never assigned. They look like copy-paste or stub scaffolding that was never closed out.

add.header: Strict TS with unknown-first boundaries
add.description: tsconfig.json enables strict mode and production modules consistently use `unknown` (not `any`) for caught errors and inbound JSON — logger.ts, clientLog.ts, parseFightRequestBody, executeWclQuery, fights and player-details handlers all narrow `unknown` before exposing typed data, so the typed core is genuinely typed.

add.header: Generic WCL request helper carries types end-to-end
add.description: src/lib/server/wclGraphQL.ts threads `<TData, TBody>` from query through transform with a custom WclQueryError, so every per-fight route handler stays in typed land without per-route casts — the boundary helper does the unsafe work once.

add.header: No enums in codebase
add.description: There are zero TypeScript `enum` declarations; domain values like roles, metrics, and spec names use string literals or `as const` arrays. The enum-bypass anti-pattern is structurally impossible here, though a few string[] could be tightened to literal unions for narrowing (e.g. role/metric in top-players).

add.header: Generic-as-promise lie at JSONB boundary
add.description: getUserRecents<T> in src/lib/db/userRecents.ts promises `RecentItem<T>[]` but never validates the JSONB `entityData` against T — the discriminated union RecentEntityData is defined right above but unused in the read path. Callers think they hold T; the runtime holds unparsed JSON.

add.header: Chart.js plugin path escapes types
add.description: damageChart.svelte uses ~7 `as any` casts to reach into Chart.js legend `this.chart` and the chartjs-plugin-annotation options. The pragma is local but recurring; the plugin's own types would remove most of them.

add.header: app.d.ts holds Workers template leftover
add.description: src/app.d.ts contains a real `export async function post(context)` body with implicit-any context — a Cloudflare Workers template fragment that doesn't belong in a SvelteKit declaration file.
